    • square now shows scale=0.700

That means PR #25 and PR #26 are actually being used in your live flow.

What matters most in these logs

1) The bigger shirt sizing is live

You can see it directly here:

  • flat...jpg → orientation=portrait scale=0.720

  • head-1684904.png → orientation=square scale=0.700

  • hot-dog-sandwich-6402792.png → orientation=square scale=0.700

So the shirt-size increase is in effect.

2) Guarded trimming is working

For two images, trimming did nothing:

  • flat...jpg stayed trimmed=750x1000

  • head-1684904.png stayed trimmed=9151x9718

That is correct, because the trim system did not falsely crop them.

For the hot dog image, trimming did happen:

  • original: 3000x2736

  • trimmed: 2846x2616

And the trim debug confirms only a modest reduction:

  • trimmed_bounds_pct=94.87x95.61

That is exactly what “guarded trimming” should do:

  • trim a little empty edge space

  • not aggressively crop the art

3) The pipeline is stable

You now have:

  • tests passing

  • rebuild flow working

  • publish/verify working

  • shirt sizing improvements applied

  • trimming safely integrated

That means the system is in a strong state.

One important interpretation

The hot dog image only trimmed a little. That means if it still looks too small on the shirt, the problem is probably not empty transparent margins alone. It is likely one of these:

  • the visible subject inside the source is still relatively small

  • the current shirt scale is still a bit conservative for that art style

  • the source art itself may need a tighter/cropped source version for best merch presentation

So if Codex is working on the next step, the most useful next enhancement is probably:

better trim diagnostics + optional trim presets, not a major pipeline rewrite.

What I would tell Codex next

Ask it to add:

  1. trim skip reasons in logs and reports

    • no alpha bounds found

    • below minimum reduction threshold

    • non-shirt template

    • trimming disabled

  2. trim aggressiveness presets

    • conservative

    • normal

    • aggressive
      while keeping numeric overrides available

  3. per-template override for shirt scale caps
    so you can test one shirt template at:

    • portrait 0.74

    • square 0.72

    • landscape 0.68
      without affecting all future templates globally

  4. optional preview artifact generation
    so you can visually compare:

    • original source

    • trimmed source

    • contained canvas

    • final placement metadata

Best practical next test after Codex

Once the next Codex changes are in, I would test only the shirt template again on the same three images, with --rebuild-product, and compare:

  • old hot dog shirt

  • current hot dog shirt

  • next tuned hot dog shirt

That will give you a very clean before/after progression.

Bottom line

Your current output says:

  • core pipeline: good

  • shirt sizing update: active

  • trim logic: working safely

  • no regression introduced

So this was a successful round. The project is now past “does it work?” and into “how do we fine-tune presentation quality?” which is exactly where you want to be.


You said:
https://github.com/infoinkvibe/inkvibe/pull/27 - steps to run a full test one shirt one mug

ChatGPT said:

PR #27 is merged. It added trim skip reporting and trim presets, while keeping shirt-only trimming guarded and leaving mug behavior unchanged. The PR notes new skip reasons like below_reduction_threshold, no_meaningful_alpha_bounds, and non_shirt_template, plus trim_bounds_preset support. GitHub

Here’s the cleanest full test for one shirt and one mug.

1) Update and verify

Run this first:

bash
git pull
python -m pytest -q

PR #27 says the full suite passed locally at 107 tests. GitHub

2) Test one shirt

This does a real rebuild, publish, and verify for exactly one shirt:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_shirt.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_shirt_failures.csv

What this does:

  • --template-key tshirt_gildan: shirt only

  • --max-artworks 1: just one image

  • --rebuild-product: applies the newest placement/trim behavior cleanly

  • --publish --verify-publish: real end-to-end test

  • --skip-audit: avoids the startup catalog audit when your template is already known-good

3) Test one mug

Then do the mug separately:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key mug_11oz \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_mug.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_mug_failures.csv

4) What success looks like

For each run, you want to see:

  • Printify product action=rebuild or create

  • Printify publish completed

  • Printify verification ok

  • run summary ending with successes=1 failures=0

For shirts after PR #27, you may also see trim logs showing either:

  • trimming applied, or

  • trimming skipped with an explicit reason like below_reduction_threshold or no_meaningful_alpha_bounds. That behavior was added in PR #27. GitHub

5) Files to check after each run

Open:

  • reports/test_one_shirt.csv

  • reports/test_one_shirt_failures.csv

  • reports/test_one_mug.csv

  • reports/test_one_mug_failures.csv

For the shirt report, look at the newer sizing/trim fields if present, especially around placement scale and trim behavior.

6) Fastest one-after-the-other sequence

If you just want the exact commands in order:

bash
git pull
python -m pytest -q
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_shirt.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_shirt_failures.csv

python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key mug_11oz \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_mug.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_mug_failures.csv

7) If you want to be extra safe first

Do the same two runs in dry-run mode before live publish:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --dry-run \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --skip-audit

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --dry-run \
  --template-key mug_11oz \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --skip-audit

Then do the live commands above.

If you paste the output from the one-shirt and one-mug live runs, I’ll tell you exactly whether both passed cleanly.

Shirt

  • template: tshirt_gildan

  • artwork: ai-generated-9228632.png

  • trimmed: 2348x3841

This is good because it shows the newer shirt improvements are actually active:

Mug

That is also good because it proves mugs stayed unchanged while shirt-only trimming behavior was added.

Plain-English breakdown of the full one-shirt / one-mug test

This is now your standard clean test flow:

Step 1

Update and verify code:

bash
git pull
python -m pytest -q

Step 2

Run one live shirt test:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_shirt.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_shirt_failures.csv

Step 3

Run one live mug test:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key mug_11oz \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_mug.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_mug_failures.csv

What to check after each run

Look for:

  • Printify product action=rebuild or create

  • Printify publish completed

  • Printify verification ok

  • successes=1 failures=0

Then check:

  • reports/test_one_shirt.csv

  • reports/test_one_shirt_failures.csv

  • reports/test_one_mug.csv

  • reports/test_one_mug_failures.csv

What this means for the project

This confirms the current baseline is strong:

  • tests pass

  • shirts work

  • mugs work

  • trimming is active only where intended

  • publish/verify works

  • rebuild works

  • reporting works

  • no regression from the recent PRs

So now you have a reliable “smoke test” for the repo:

  • one shirt

  • one mug

  • live end-to-end

Recommended next step

Use this same pattern whenever a new PR changes placement, trimming, content, or state behavior:

  • run tests

  • run one shirt

  • run one mug

  • inspect reports

  • then scale up

If you want, I can turn this into a permanent testing checklist / runbook you can save in the repo.

You said:
$ Run summary ... successes=1 failures=0 ... products_rebuilt=1 ... publish_attempts=1 publish_verified=1 bash: Run: command not found

ChatGPT said:

That happened because Bash tried to execute this as a command:

text
Run summary ... successes=1 failures=0 ... products_rebuilt=1 ... publish_attempts=1 publish_verified=1

It’s not a command — it’s just a log line from the pipeline output.

So:

  • your shirt run already finished successfully

  • nothing is wrong with InkVibeAuto there

  • Bash is only complaining because that text was entered at the prompt

Use the mug command next, exactly like this:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key mug_11oz \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_mug.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_mug_failures.csv

The important part from your shirt run was already this result:

  • successes=1

  • failures=0

  • products_rebuilt=1

  • publish_attempts=1

  • publish_verified=1

So the shirt test passed. Now just run the mug command.

What this confirms

Your current main branch is in a good state:

  • repo is up to date

  • tests pass

  • shirt flow works

  • verify-publish works

Your standard full test, one shirt + one mug

This is now the clean test sequence to reuse after future PRs:

bash
git pull
python -m pytest -q

python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_shirt.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_shirt_failures.csv

python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key mug_11oz \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_mug.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_mug_failures.csv

How to read success quickly

For each run, the key line is the summary:

  • successes=1

  • failures=0

  • products_rebuilt=1

  • publish_attempts=1

  • publish_verified=1

That means the run passed.

Recommended next move

Now that the baseline smoke test is confirmed, the next practical thing is to:

  • keep this as your repeatable regression test

  • move on to the next enhancement branch with confidence

The project is now in a solid place operationally.

  • repo current

Your green-light indicators are:

  • 107 passed

  • shirt summary: successes=1 failures=0 publish_verified=1

  • mug summary: successes=1 failures=0 publish_verified=1

At this point, I’d treat this as the standard validation sequence after each PR:

bash
git pull
python -m pytest -q

python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_shirt.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_shirt_failures.csv

python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key mug_11oz \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_mug.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_mug_failures.csv

Next best move is to put this into the repo as a short TESTING.md or README section so the workflow stays consistent.

ChatGPT said:

Use this for TESTING.md:

markdown
# TESTING

This project uses a quick live smoke test to confirm the current Printify pipeline is still working after code changes.

The current known-good baseline covers:

- `tshirt_gildan`
- `mug_11oz`
- rebuild flow
- publish flow
- verify-publish flow
- state persistence
- run/failure report export

## Prerequisites

Before running tests, make sure:

- your repo is up to date
- your Python environment is active
- dependencies are installed
- `.env` is configured
- `PRINTIFY_API_TOKEN` is valid
- `PRINTIFY_SHOP_ID` is valid
- `product_templates.json` is present
- `images/` contains at least one valid artwork file

## 1. Pull latest code

```bash
git pull

2. Run automated tests

bash
python -m pytest -q

Expected result:

  • all tests pass

  • current baseline is 107 passed or higher as the suite grows

3. Live smoke test: one shirt

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_shirt.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_shirt_failures.csv

Shirt pass criteria

The run should end with:

  • successes=1

  • failures=0

  • products_rebuilt=1

  • publish_attempts=1

  • publish_verified=1

Expected behavior:

  • contain fit mode is preserved

  • shirt trimming may run if enabled by template settings

  • run report is written

  • failure report is written with rows=0

4. Live smoke test: one mug

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key mug_11oz \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_mug.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_mug_failures.csv

Mug pass criteria

The run should end with:

  • successes=1

  • failures=0

  • products_rebuilt=1

  • publish_attempts=1

  • publish_verified=1

Expected behavior:

  • contain fit mode is preserved

  • mug trimming should remain unchanged unless explicitly configured later

  • run report is written

  • failure report is written with rows=0

5. Files to inspect

After the smoke test, check:

  • reports/test_one_shirt.csv

  • reports/test_one_shirt_failures.csv

  • reports/test_one_mug.csv

  • reports/test_one_mug_failures.csv

Expected:

  • both run reports contain 1 row

  • both failure reports contain 0 rows

6. Standard smoke test command sequence

bash
git pull
python -m pytest -q

python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_shirt.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_shirt_failures.csv

python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key mug_11oz \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_mug.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_mug_failures.csv

7. When to use this

Run this smoke test after:

  • merging a PR

  • changing image placement logic

  • changing trimming logic

  • changing title/content generation logic that could affect payloads

  • changing state/resume logic

  • changing product template behavior

  • changing upload behavior

8. Troubleshooting

--placement-preview not recognized

Your local checkout does not include that CLI flag. Use the documented smoke test commands above.

Stored product not found / 404

This usually means state.json points to an old deleted product. The pipeline should recover automatically by creating or rebuilding a new product.

Variant mismatch / HTTP 400

This usually means the stored product or payload is incompatible with the current blueprint/provider/print area setup. Retry with --rebuild-product.

Audit slows startup or hits rate limits

Use --skip-audit once the template is already known-good.

9. Current baseline expectation

A healthy baseline run looks like this:

  • tests pass

  • one shirt live run succeeds

  • one mug live run succeeds

  • both publish successfully

  • both verify successfully

  • both failure reports are empty

If all of those are true, the current baseline is considered good.

css

If you want, I can also turn that into a shorter README section version.

So the shirt side is good.

ChatGPT said:

That passed too.

So the full live smoke test is green:

  • shirt: passed

  • mug: passed

  • both rebuilt

  • both published

  • both verified

  • both failure reports empty

Pass indicators from the mug run:

  • status=success

  • action=rebuild

  • rows=0 in reports/test_one_mug_failures.csv

  • summary shows successes=1 failures=0 publish_verified=1

You now have a confirmed repeatable baseline for:

  • tshirt_gildan

  • mug_11oz

  • rebuild

  • publish

  • verify-publish

  • report export

  • shirt trim behavior

  • mug unchanged behavior

That’s a solid stopping point before the next feature branch.


You said:
command for full test for 9 shirts and 9 mugs.

ChatGPT said:

Use these for a full live batch test of 9 shirts and 9 mugs.

9 shirts

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --max-artworks 9 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_nine_shirts.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_nine_shirts_failures.csv

9 mugs

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key mug_11oz \
  --max-artworks 9 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_nine_mugs.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_nine_mugs_failures.csv

If you want a safer controlled batch, use --batch-size 3 too:

9 shirts in batches of 3

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --max-artworks 9 \
  --batch-size 3 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_nine_shirts.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_nine_shirts_failures.csv

9 mugs in batches of 3

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key mug_11oz \
  --max-artworks 9 \
  --batch-size 3 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_nine_mugs.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_nine_mugs_failures.csv

If you want the safest version, add --stop-after-failures 1 so it halts on the first bad item.


You said:
safe version - for 9 cups and 9 mugs - one solid command

ChatGPT said:

Use this safe combined command for 9 shirts and 9 mugs in one run:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --template-key mug_11oz \
  --max-artworks 9 \
  --batch-size 3 \
  --stop-after-failures 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_nine_shirts_nine_mugs.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_nine_shirts_nine_mugs_failures.csv

What it does:

  • processes up to 9 artwork files

  • runs them through both templates

  • gives you up to 18 total products: 9 shirts + 9 mugs

  • works in smaller batches of 3

  • stops after the first failure

  • rebuilds, publishes, and verifies each product

If you meant 9 mugs only and not shirts, say that and I’ll give you the single-template command.

ChatGPT said:

It ran safely, but it did not do 9 shirts + 9 mugs.

It only processed 3 total combinations, which your summary shows here:

  • artworks_scanned=9

  • templates_processed=3

  • combinations_processed=3

So the limiter was --batch-size 3.

In your pipeline, --batch-size is limiting the number of combinations processed in that run, not “3 artworks per template.” That’s why it stopped after:

  1. shirt for artwork 1

  2. mug for artwork 1

  3. shirt for artwork 2

and then ended.

Use this instead for a safe full 9 shirts + 9 mugs run

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --template-key mug_11oz \
  --max-artworks 9 \
  --stop-after-failures 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_nine_shirts_nine_mugs.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_nine_shirts_nine_mugs_failures.csv

That will allow up to:

  • 9 artworks

  • × 2 templates

  • = 18 total combinations

If you want safer chunking without cutting it short

Set --batch-size 18:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --template-key mug_11oz \
  --max-artworks 9 \
  --batch-size 18 \
  --stop-after-failures 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_nine_shirts_nine_mugs.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_nine_shirts_nine_mugs_failures.csv

That preserves the explicit batch flag but still lets all 18 combinations run.

One more note from your logs

These warnings are not fatal:

  • Request DELETE ... failed with HTTP 404

  • Delete existing product failed during rebuild

That just means the old product ID was already gone before rebuild delete happened. The pipeline recovered correctly by rebuilding a fresh product afterward.

So the run was healthy — it was just shorter than intended because of --batch-size 3.

ChatGPT said:

That’s the full success result.

What happened

The run completed the full intended batch:

  • 9 artworks scanned

  • 2 templates

  • 18 total combinations processed

  • 18 successes

  • 0 failures

  • 18 rebuilt

  • 18 published

  • 18 verified

The key line is:

text
Run summary artworks_scanned=9 templates_processed=18 combinations_processed=18 successes=18 failures=0 ... publish_attempts=18 publish_verified=18

So this command is the correct safe full-batch command

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --template-key mug_11oz \
  --max-artworks 9 \
  --stop-after-failures 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_nine_shirts_nine_mugs.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_nine_shirts_nine_mugs_failures.csv

What the logs also show

A few DELETE ... 404 warnings appeared during rebuild. Those were not fatal.

That means:

  • the old product ID stored in state no longer existed

  • rebuild delete tried first

  • delete got 404

  • pipeline recovered by creating/rebuilding a fresh product

  • publish and verification still succeeded

So those warnings are acceptable in this flow.

Exported files

Your run produced:

  • reports/test_nine_shirts_nine_mugs.csv → 18 rows

  • reports/test_nine_shirts_nine_mugs_failures.csv → 0 rows

Bottom line

You now have two proven commands:

Small smoke test

1 shirt + 1 mug

Safe larger validation

9 shirts + 9 mugs in one run

Both are working. The 9+9 command is now your trusted larger regression test.


You said:
shirt images are still too small. lets take a alook at everything that happens to the image from images folder - at some point - it switches size / somewhere.. anayzlye this and lets get it resolved.

ChatGPT said:

I’m tracing the image path end to end now: from the source file in images/, through trimming, canvas export, orientation detection, placement scaling, and the final Printify payload. The likely issue is a compound one, not a single resize.

I found the main cause. The image is getting “smaller” in two different stages, and the first one is the big one: if the source art is smaller than the shirt canvas, the pipeline keeps it at original size and centers it on a 4500×5400 transparent canvas because allow_upscale is false and artwork_fit_mode is contain. Then Printify applies a second placement scale on that already-padded canvas.

Yes — I see where it “switches size,” and it is happening in two separate places, not one.

What happens to an image from images/

  1. The source file is opened, EXIF-rotated, and converted to RGBA. Then the pipeline may trim bounds, but that trim is based on the alpha channel. If the image is an opaque JPG, or an opaque PNG without transparent margins, trimming usually does nothing. GitHub+1

  2. The pipeline then builds a full placement canvas for the product:

    • shirts: 4500 x 5400

    • mugs: 2700 x 1120

    For contain, it computes a resize scale using the source/trimmed image and the required canvas. But when allow_upscale is false, that scale is clamped to <= 1.0, so smaller source art is not enlarged. Instead, it is pasted centered onto a much larger transparent canvas. That is the first big shrink effect. Your shirt template currently has allow_upscale: false and artwork_fit_mode: "contain". GitHub+2GitHub+2

  3. After that export is created, the Printify payload applies a second scale using the placement transform. For shirts, the code caps that by orientation at:

    • portrait 0.72

    • square 0.70

    • landscape 0.66

    and it uses min(template placement_scale, orientation_cap). Your shirt template has placement_scale: 0.9, so the runtime cap wins. That means the already-padded canvas gets scaled again. GitHub+2GitHub+2

So the real flow is:

source image → optional alpha trim → centered onto big transparent export canvas without upscaling → Printify applies another placement scale

That is why some shirt images still look small. GitHub+3GitHub+3GitHub+3

Why your small images look especially tiny

Your own logs show it clearly.

For cat-697522...png, the shirt path became:

  • original 500x500

  • trimmed 433x465

  • final canvas 4500x5400

  • upscaled=False

  • placement scale 0.700

So the visible art is only about 9.6% of canvas width (433 / 4500) and 8.6% of canvas height (465 / 5400) before the Printify transform is even applied. That is why it will look tiny on a shirt.

For flat,750x...jpg, it stayed:

  • original 750x1000

  • trimmed 750x1000

  • final canvas 4500x5400

  • upscaled=False

  • placement scale 0.720

That is still only about 16.7% of canvas width and 18.5% of canvas height before the placement transform. The image is being preserved, but it is not being filled.

The important root cause

The biggest issue is not the Printify scale cap by itself.

The biggest issue is this combination:

  • artwork_fit_mode = contain

  • allow_upscale = false

  • trim only uses alpha bounds

  • then a second placement scale is applied

That works great for avoiding cropping, but it makes small or opaque source images look small on shirts. GitHub+2GitHub+2

What to do right now

The fastest proof test is this:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --allow-upscale \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_shirt_upscaled.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_shirt_upscaled_failures.csv

If that one shirt suddenly looks much better, then you have confirmed the main issue: the export stage is too conservative, not just the placement transform.

What I recommend as the real fix

I would do this in order:

  1. Keep contain for shirts. That avoids the earlier cropping problem.

  2. Add shirt-only controlled upscaling, not unlimited upscaling.

  3. Add a better trim mode for opaque images, not just alpha-trim.

  4. Report a new metric like subject fill ratio so you can see how much of the 4500x5400 canvas the art actually occupies.

Best long-term code change

The safest real solution is:

  • For shirts only, after trim and before export, compute the visible subject fill ratio.

  • If the subject occupies less than a target threshold, upscale it to hit a target fill ratio.

  • Cap the upscale so low-res art does not get blown up too far.

  • Keep mugs unchanged.

That would solve the actual problem instead of just pushing the placement scale harder.

Next Codex prompt

text
We traced the current shirt sizing issue in InkVibeAuto. The problem is not a single resize step; it is a compound effect:

1. Source artwork is opened, EXIF-transposed, converted to RGBA.
2. Optional trimming is currently alpha-based only, so opaque JPGs and opaque PNGs usually do not trim.
3. For contain mode, the pipeline exports to the full shirt canvas (4500x5400), but when allow_upscale is false it clamps scale to <= 1.0, so small art stays small and is centered on a large transparent canvas.
4. Printify then applies a second placement transform scale (shirt caps currently portrait=0.72, square=0.70, landscape=0.66), so the already-padded art is effectively scaled again.

Goal:
Fix shirt images still appearing too small, without regressing current working flows.

Please implement the safest enhancement plan:

Phase 1: shirt-only controlled upscale
1. Add template-level shirt-only controlled upscale support for contain mode.
2. Introduce settings like:
   - subject_fill_target_pct
   - max_upscale_factor
   - upscale_only_if_subject_fill_below_pct
3. Apply this only to shirt-like templates by default.
4. Keep mugs unchanged.

Phase 2: better trimming for opaque images
1. Add an optional trim mode for opaque images, not just alpha-based trimming.
2. Support modes like:
   - alpha
   - alpha_or_solid_bg
   - off
3. Preserve current behavior by default unless enabled.

Phase 3: diagnostics
1. Add report/log fields for:
   - subject_fill_pct_before
   - subject_fill_pct_after
   - effective_upscale_factor
   - trim_mode_used
2. Keep changes additive.

Phase 4: tests
1. Add regression coverage for shirt-only controlled upscale.
2. Add tests for opaque-image trimming.
3. Add tests proving mugs remain unchanged.
4. Keep publish, verify, resume, and state behavior intact.

Constraints:
- Do not remove contain mode.
- Do not change mug defaults.
- Do not regress working shirt/mug create-publish-verify flows.
- Preserve current smoke-test commands.

If you want, I can also turn this into a plain-English “why it shrinks” diagram with your exact example images.

ChatGPT said:

I’m pulling the current pipeline and template files so I can point to the exact config and code paths causing the shirt shrink effect, then I’ll give you the fastest fix to test before any more code changes.

You found the real problem: the shirt art is shrinking in the export stage, then getting scaled again in the placement stage.

What is happening

Your shirt template currently uses:

  • a shirt placement canvas of 4500×5400

  • artwork_fit_mode: "contain"

  • allow_upscale: false

  • placement_scale: 0.9 on the template side, while the runtime shirt cap is what you’re seeing in logs like 0.720 / 0.700 / 0.660. GitHub+1

The resize code does this for contain:

  • compute the fit scale from source image to required canvas

  • clamp scale to <= 1.0 when upscaling is not allowed

  • resize the image

  • paste it centered into a full transparent 4500×5400 canvas (contained_padded) GitHub

That means small source art does not get enlarged. It just gets dropped into a huge shirt canvas with lots of transparent space around it, and then the placement transform is applied on top of that. GitHub

Your logs show it clearly

These are the strongest examples from your run:

  • cat-697522...png
    original 500x500 → trimmed 433x465 → final canvas 4500x5400 → upscaled=False → shirt placement scale 0.700

  • flat,750x...jpg
    original 750x1000 → trimmed 750x1000 → final canvas 4500x5400 → upscaled=False → shirt placement scale 0.720

  • good-night-1857356_1920.png
    original 1920x1657 → trimmed unchanged → final canvas 4500x5400 → upscaled=False

So yes: the visible art is being preserved, but it is being preserved too small inside the shirt export.

The fastest fix to test

Turn on upscale for shirts.

The code path already supports this through options.allow_upscale or placement.allow_upscale. GitHub

Best immediate test

Run shirts only, with upscale enabled:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --max-artworks 9 \
  --allow-upscale \
  --stop-after-failures 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_nine_shirts_upscaled.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_nine_shirts_upscaled_failures.csv

This is the fastest way to prove the diagnosis without changing code first.

The better permanent fix

Change only the shirt template in product_templates.json:

Current shirt placement

  • allow_upscale: false

  • artwork_fit_mode: "contain" GitHub

Change it to

json
"allow_upscale": true

Leave mugs alone.

That gives you:

  • shirts can fill the shirt canvas better

  • mugs stay as they are

  • contain still prevents cropping

What I would not change yet

Do not remove contain.
That would risk bringing back the earlier crop problem.

Do not change mugs yet.
The mug flow looks healthy.

Do not keep chasing placement scale alone.
The logs show the bigger loss is happening before placement, when small art is centered on a large shirt canvas without being enlarged.

Best next move

I’d do this in order:

  1. Run the 9-shirt test with --allow-upscale

  2. Compare those shirt mockups against the current ones

  3. If that fixes the size problem, make allow_upscale: true permanent for tshirt_gildan

  4. Keep mugs unchanged

Codex prompt

text
We traced the shirt size issue in InkVibeAuto and identified the main cause.

Current behavior:
- shirt template uses artwork_fit_mode="contain"
- shirt placement canvas is 4500x5400
- shirt placement currently has allow_upscale=false
- contain export logic clamps scale to <= 1.0 when allow_upscale is false
- then it pastes the resized image into a full transparent 4500x5400 canvas
- Printify then applies the placement transform scale afterward

Result:
Small source images stay small and are centered inside a large transparent shirt canvas before placement transform, making shirt art look too small.

Goal:
Fix shirt image sizing without regressing mugs or reintroducing cropping.

Please implement the safest enhancement plan:

1. Set tshirt_gildan placement allow_upscale to true in product_templates.json.
2. Keep mugs unchanged.
3. Keep artwork_fit_mode="contain".
4. Add regression tests proving shirt exports upscale when enabled and mugs remain unchanged.
5. Add report/log fields for effective upscale factor if not already present.
6. Update README/TESTING docs to note that shirt exports now allow controlled upscale while mugs remain conservative.

Constraints:
- Do not remove contain.
- Do not change mug defaults.
- Do not regress publish/verify/rebuild behavior.
- Keep changes additive and safe.

If you want the most careful test first, run the same shirt command with --max-artworks 1 on the cat-697522...png image before doing all 9.


You said:
$ 6python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \ --template-key tshirt_gildan \ --max-artworks 9 \ --allow-upscale \ --stop-after-failures 1 \ --rebuild-product \ --publish \ --verify-publish \ --skip-audit \ --export-run-report reports/test_nine_shirts_upscaled.csv \ --export-failure-report reports/test_nine_shirts_upscaled_failures.csv bash: 6python: command not found

ChatGPT said:

You have a typo.

You entered:

bash
6python

Bash tried to run a command literally named 6python, which does not exist.

Use this exact command instead:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --max-artworks 9 \
  --allow-upscale \
  --stop-after-failures 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_nine_shirts_upscaled.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_nine_shirts_upscaled_failures.csv

The only fix is removing the 6 before python.

Once you run that, the important thing to watch for in the logs is whether lines that used to say:

text
upscaled=False

now become:

text
upscaled=True

or show a larger resized= value for the shirt export. That will tell us whether the shirt image is finally being enlarged before it gets placed.


You said:
Summary Enabled controlled per-placement upscale for tshirt_gildan front (allow_upscale: true) while keeping mug defaults unchanged (allow_upscale: false), with both templates still using artwork_fit_mode: "contain". Added additive upscale-factor reporting plumbing: New effective_upscale_factor fields in resolution/prepared/run-report models. Contain/cover resolution logs now emit the effective upscale factor. Run reporting now captures and exports the effective upscale factor per processed row. Added regression tests proving the requested behavior: shirt template allows upscale while mug remains conservative, shirt contain export upscales undersized input, mug contain export does not. Updated documentation to note per-placement allow_upscale, the shirt-vs-mug default split, and the run report effective_upscale_factor field. Testing ✅ pytest -q tests/test_pipeline.py -k "shirt_template_enables_allow_upscale_while_mug_stays_conservative or shirt_contain_export_upscales_but_mug_contain_export_does_not or run_batch_size_and_resume_and_reporting or contain_mode_preserves_aspect_ratio_when_upscaled" ✅ pytest -q tests/test_pipeline.py Committed on current branch: 9a5c7a6 Files (4) README.md README.md +3 -0 printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py +16 -4 product_templates.json product_templates.json +1 -1 test_pipeline.py tests/test_pipeline.py +35 -0

ChatGPT said:

Good. That is the right change.

You’ve now changed the actual root cause for shirts:

  • tshirt_gildan can now upscale during contain export

  • mug_11oz stays conservative

  • both still use contain

  • you now have effective_upscale_factor in logs and reports

What to run next

Run shirts only first:

bash
git pull
python -m pytest -q

python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --max-artworks 9 \
  --stop-after-failures 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_nine_shirts_upscaled.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_nine_shirts_upscaled_failures.csv

What you want to see in the logs

Before, shirt logs were showing things like:

  • upscaled=False

  • small resized= values like 433x465, 750x1000, 1920x1657

Now you want to see:

  • upscaled=True on undersized shirt images

  • larger resized= values

  • an effective_upscale_factor greater than 1.0

Examples of the images most likely to prove the fix:

  • cat-697522...png

  • flat,750x...jpg

  • good-night-1857356_1920.png

Those were all previously staying too small.

What to check in the report

Open:

text
reports/test_nine_shirts_upscaled.csv

Look for the new field:

  • effective_upscale_factor

For small shirt images, you want that field to be above 1.0.

After that

If the shirts now look right, do the full 9 shirts + 9 mugs regression again:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --template-key mug_11oz \
  --max-artworks 9 \
  --stop-after-failures 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_nine_shirts_nine_mugs_upscaled.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_nine_shirts_nine_mugs_upscaled_failures.csv

What success looks like

For the shirt-only run:

  • successes=9

  • failures=0

For the full combined run:

  • successes=18

  • failures=0

And visually, the shirt images that were previously tiny should now fill more of the shirt.

The best next thing is to paste one or two shirt log sections after this change, especially for cat-697522...png or flat,750x...jpg, so we can confirm the upscale is actually kicking in.

ChatGPT said:

This confirms the fix worked.

What changed in the shirt pipeline:

  • before, small shirt images were centered on a 4500x5400 canvas without enlargement

  • now, undersized shirt art is being upscaled during the contain export step

  • mugs stayed unchanged

Your logs prove it:

  • ai-generated-9228632.png → upscaled=True, effective_upscale_factor=1.406, resized to 3302x5400

  • cat-697522...png → upscaled=True, effective_upscale_factor=10.393, resized to 4500x4833

  • flat,750x...jpg → upscaled=True, effective_upscale_factor=5.400, resized to 4051x5400

  • good-night-1857356_1920.png → upscaled=True, effective_upscale_factor=2.344, resized to 4500x3884

  • hot-dog-sandwich...png → upscaled=True, effective_upscale_factor=1.581, resized to 4500x4137

  • yang-38737_1920.png → upscaled=True, effective_upscale_factor=2.344, resized to 4500x4500

And for already-large art, it correctly did not upscale:

  • head-1684904.png → effective_upscale_factor=1.000

  • sunflower-2511961.jpg → effective_upscale_factor=1.000

So the root cause was real, and this change addressed it.

Also:

  • successes=9

  • failures=0

  • publish_verified=9

That means the shirt-only upscale change is operationally clean.

One thing to watch now is quality, not size:

  • the cat image was upscaled by 10.393x

  • that should make it fill the shirt much better, but it may look soft or blurry because the source was only 500x500

So the problem has shifted from “too small” to possibly “too enlarged” on low-res art.

Best next move:

  • visually inspect the 9 shirt mockups

  • if they now look the right size, the main sizing issue is solved

  • if a few look blurry, the next enhancement should be a max upscale cap for shirts, not another sizing rewrite

If you want, I can help you define the next safe rule set for shirt quality, like:

  • upscale shirts, but cap at something like 3x or 4x

  • skip or warn on very low-resolution sources

  • keep current behavior for already-large art


You said:

ChatGPT said:

This confirms the sizing fix is working in production.

What changed

The full 9 shirts + 9 mugs run now shows exactly what we wanted:

Shirts

Undersized shirt images are now being enlarged during the contain export step.

Examples from your logs:

  • ai-generated-9228632.png → upscaled=True, effective_upscale_factor=1.406

  • cat-697522...png → upscaled=True, effective_upscale_factor=10.393

  • flat,750x...jpg → upscaled=True, effective_upscale_factor=5.400

  • good-night-1857356_1920.png → upscaled=True, effective_upscale_factor=2.344

  • hot-dog-sandwich...png → upscaled=True, effective_upscale_factor=1.581

  • yang-38737_1920.png → upscaled=True, effective_upscale_factor=2.344

Large shirt art stayed unchanged:

  • head-1684904.png → effective_upscale_factor=1.000

  • sunflower-2511961.jpg → effective_upscale_factor=1.000

Mugs

Mugs stayed conservative, exactly as intended:

  • every mug row shows upscaled=False

  • effective_upscale_factor=1.000

So the split is now correct:

  • shirts upscale when needed

  • mugs do not

Operational result

The run itself was fully successful:

  • artworks_scanned=9

  • templates_processed=18

  • combinations_processed=18

  • successes=18

  • failures=0

  • publish_attempts=18

  • publish_verified=18

So this change did not break the pipeline.

What this means

The original “shirt images are too small” issue was real, and this change addressed the actual cause:

  • before: small art was centered on a giant shirt canvas without enlargement

  • now: small shirt art is enlarged before placement

That is the right fix.

The new thing to watch

Now the likely issue is not size, but quality for very low-resolution art.

The clearest example is:

  • cat-697522...png → effective_upscale_factor=10.393

That will make it much bigger on the shirt, but it may also make it look soft or blurry.

So the next safe enhancement is probably:

Add a max upscale cap for shirts

Examples:

  • cap at 3.0x or 4.0x

  • warn when art would need more than that

  • optionally skip or flag very low-resolution images for manual review

Best next Codex task

text
We fixed the shirt-sizing issue by enabling controlled contain-mode upscale for shirts only, while keeping mugs conservative. The pipeline is working and must not regress.

Current confirmed behavior:
- shirts now upscale when undersized
- mugs remain unchanged
- full 9-shirt + 9-mug run succeeded with 18/18 verified
- new effective_upscale_factor reporting is working

New goal:
Improve shirt image quality by preventing extreme upscale on very low-resolution art.

Please implement the safest next enhancement plan:

1. Add a shirt-only max_upscale_factor setting at template/placement level.
2. Default it conservatively (for example 3.0 or 4.0).
3. If the required upscale exceeds the cap:
   - clamp to the cap
   - emit a warning in logs
   - include warning/report fields showing requested vs applied upscale
4. Add report/log fields:
   - requested_upscale_factor
   - applied_upscale_factor
   - upscale_capped (true/false)
5. Add regression tests proving:
   - shirts upscale but respect the cap
   - mugs remain unchanged
   - current publish/verify/rebuild flows are not affected

Constraints:
- keep contain mode
- keep mug defaults unchanged
- preserve current smoke tests and full-batch workflow
- keep changes additive and safe

Bottom line: the shirt-size problem is now fixed. The next refinement is controlling how much low-res shirt art is allowed to upscale.


You said:
https://github.com/infoinkvibe/inkvibe/pull/29Summary Added new additive shirt-only upscale cap support by introducing max_upscale_factor on both placement and template models, plus new reporting fields (requested_upscale_factor, applied_upscale_factor, upscale_capped) in resolution/prepared/run-report dataclasses. Implemented safe cap behavior in artwork resolution: compute requested upscale, clamp to cap when exceeded, emit warning logs, and carry requested/applied/capped values through to output while preserving effective_upscale_factor as the applied value. Wired shirt-only cap resolution into export prep (placement override -> template default, shirts only), and propagated new values into PreparedArtwork. Added template validation/loading support for max_upscale_factor at both placement and template levels (must be > 0 when provided). Extended run-report population to include requested_upscale_factor, applied_upscale_factor, and upscale_capped across skipped/success/failure rows. Updated product_templates.json with conservative shirt defaults (max_upscale_factor: 4.0) at template + shirt placement level; mug remains unchanged/conservative. Added docs for new placement option and expanded run-report fields. Added/updated regression tests proving shirt upscale is capped, mugs stay unchanged, invalid cap validation is enforced, and publish/verify/rebuild flows remain covered and passing. Testing ✅ pytest -q tests/test_pipeline.py Committed on current branch: 7f6979f Files (4) README.md README.md +3 -2 printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py +75 -9 product_templates.json product_templates.json +4 -2 test_pipeline.py tests/test_pipeline.py +65 -1-

ChatGPT said:

I’m checking PR #29 against the live repo so I can give you the exact next validation run for the new shirt upscale cap, not just a generic suggestion.

PR #29 is the right next step. It adds a shirt-only max_upscale_factor cap, with template/placement support, new run-report fields for requested vs applied upscale, and a default shirt cap of 4.0 while leaving mugs unchanged. GitHub

One important note: the shirt logs you pasted earlier with values like effective_upscale_factor=10.393 for the cat image are from before this cap was applied. After PR #29, that kind of shirt should be clamped and logged as capped rather than actually using the full requested upscale. GitHub

Run this next

First update and run tests:

bash
git pull
python -m pytest -q

Then rerun shirts only so you can verify the cap behavior clearly:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --max-artworks 9 \
  --stop-after-failures 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_nine_shirts_capped.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_nine_shirts_capped_failures.csv

If that looks good, rerun the combined batch:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --template-key mug_11oz \
  --max-artworks 9 \
  --stop-after-failures 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_nine_shirts_nine_mugs_capped.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_nine_shirts_nine_mugs_capped_failures.csv

What you should see now

For very small shirt art, look for:

  • a warning that the requested upscale exceeded the cap

  • requested_upscale_factor greater than 4.0

  • applied_upscale_factor equal to 4.0

  • upscale_capped=true

For mugs, you should still see:

  • effective_upscale_factor=1.000

  • no shirt-style upscale behavior

That matches the PR description: shirt-only cap, conservative mug behavior, and additive reporting fields. GitHub

What this should solve

This should move you from:

  • too small shirts
    to

  • better-sized shirts without extreme 10x blowups

So the likely outcome is:

  • cat-like tiny images become larger than before

  • but no longer get absurdly enlarged

  • mugs remain stable

Paste one capped shirt log section after the rerun, especially for the cat image, and I’ll tell you whether the cap is behaving exactly as intended.

ChatGPT said:

I found the key issue from your latest run: the new upscale cap is not actually being applied yet in the code you just executed. The giveaway is the cat image still showing effective_upscale_factor=10.393; with a shirt cap of 4.0, that should not happen.

This run is not using PR #29 yet.

Why I’m sure:

  • PR #29 is merged into main and says capped shirt upscales should log requested_upscale_factor, applied_upscale_factor, and upscale_capped, with a default shirt cap of 4.0. GitHub

  • Your log still shows the old uncapped behavior, for example:

    • cat-697522... → effective_upscale_factor=10.393

  • If PR #29 were active, that cat image should be clamped near 4.0, not 10.393, and you should see the new cap-related fields/warnings in the logs. GitHub

So the fix is simple: pull latest first, then rerun.

Use this:

bash
git pull
python -m pytest -q

python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --max-artworks 9 \
  --stop-after-failures 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_nine_shirts_capped.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_nine_shirts_capped_failures.csv

What you should see after the pull:

  • small shirt images no longer using giant upscales like 10.393

  • cap-related reporting fields present

  • cat/flat-style images clamped to the configured shirt cap instead of unlimited enlargement. GitHub

Then rerun the combined batch:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --template-key mug_11oz \
  --max-artworks 9 \
  --stop-after-failures 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_nine_shirts_nine_mugs_capped.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_nine_shirts_nine_mugs_capped_failures.csv

Paste the next shirt log after git pull, especially the cat row, and I’ll check whether the cap is finally active.

ChatGPT said:

Perfect — PR #29 is active now, and the cap is working.

What changed

The log now shows the new capped behavior exactly as intended.

Cap is active

For cat-697522...png:

  • requested_upscale_factor=10.393

  • applied_upscale_factor=4.000

  • upscale_capped=True

For flat,750x...jpg:

  • requested_upscale_factor=5.400

  • applied_upscale_factor=4.000

  • upscale_capped=True

So the old runaway shirt blowups are now being limited.

Normal upscales still work

These were below the cap and stayed uncapped:

  • ai-generated-9228632.png → 1.406

  • good-night-1857356_1920.png → 2.344

  • hot-dog-sandwich...png → 1.581

  • spare-ribs...png → 2.344

  • yang-38737_1920.png → 2.344

Large art stays unchanged

These correctly did not upscale:

  • head-1684904.png → 1.000

  • sunflower-2511961.jpg → 1.000

Operational result

The run was fully clean:

  • successes=9

  • failures=0

  • publish_verified=9

So now the shirt behavior is in a much better place:

  • not too small anymore

  • not absurdly over-upscaled either

What this means

You now have the right shirt sizing pipeline behavior:

  • trim if useful

  • contain mode preserved

  • upscale only when needed

  • cap extreme upscale at 4.0

  • keep large source art unchanged

That is the correct balance between:

  • size

  • safety

  • quality

Next best step

Now run the full combined capped regression:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --template-key mug_11oz \
  --max-artworks 9 \
  --stop-after-failures 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_nine_shirts_nine_mugs_capped.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_nine_shirts_nine_mugs_capped_failures.csv

If that finishes cleanly, then you’ve got a strong new stable baseline:

  • shirts sized better

  • low-res shirts capped safely

  • mugs unchanged

  • full batch verified end to end.

ChatGPT said:

You hit a different issue now: not image sizing, but a Printify rate-limit stall. I’m checking the current repo code to see whether there’s already a way to avoid that long 429 retry on the mug catalog/variant lookup path.

That’s the result you wanted.

What this confirms

The full capped run is behaving correctly:

  • shirts are upscaling when needed

  • extreme shirt upscales are being capped

  • mugs remain conservative

  • the batch is still publishing and verifying successfully

From your log:

  • cat-697522...png

    • requested_upscale_factor=10.393

    • applied_upscale_factor=4.000

    • upscale_capped=True

  • flat,750x...jpg

    • requested_upscale_factor=5.400

    • applied_upscale_factor=4.000

    • upscale_capped=True

  • normal shirt images still upscale below the cap:

    • ai-generated... → 1.406

    • good-night... → 2.344

    • hot-dog... → 1.581

  • large shirt art stays unchanged:

    • head-1684904.png → 1.000

    • sunflower-2511961.jpg → 1.000

  • mugs stay unchanged:

    • upscaled=False

    • effective_upscale_factor=1.000

That matches what PR #29 added: shirt-only max_upscale_factor, requested vs applied upscale reporting, and conservative mug behavior. GitHub

Operational result

Your combined run is clean:

  • artworks_scanned=9

  • templates_processed=18

  • combinations_processed=18

  • successes=18

  • failures=0

  • publish_attempts=18

  • publish_verified=18

So you now have a stronger baseline than before:

  • shirt sizing fixed

  • low-res shirt blowups capped

  • mugs unchanged

  • full 9+9 batch verified end to end

Bottom line

The shirt image sizing issue is now in a good place:

  • no longer too small

  • no longer wildly over-upscaled

  • still fully operational in live runs

The next improvement, if needed, is not sizing logic anymore. It would be quality policy, like flagging very low-res shirt art for manual review when it hits the 4.0 cap.


You said:
this image - of the shirt - the cat image - the cat is too small. the actual artwork / design is too small . the white space may be big but not the design image itself.

ChatGPT said:

You said:

ChatGPT said:

Yes — the screenshot confirms it.

The design itself is too small on the shirt, not just the outer image box.

What that means:

  • the recent fixes improved the export canvas sizing

  • but for this cat file, the pipeline is still scaling the outer detected bounds, not the truly visible cat/text subject

  • so even after upscale, the actual artwork stays tiny in the middle

In other words:

source file has a small visible subject → trim is not tight enough → upscale is applied to the wrong box → shirt still looks tiny

For this specific cat image, the current log already hinted at it:

  • original: 500x500

  • trimmed: 433x465

That trim is only a little tighter, but the visible cat in the mockup is much smaller than a 433x465 subject should look. So the trim is not finding the real subject tightly enough.

Best immediate fix

For this artwork, manually crop the original source much tighter around:

  • the cat

  • the text above it

Then rerun the shirt.

That will work faster and better than trying to force more global scaling.

Best pipeline fix

The next code change should not be “make shirts even bigger globally.”

It should be:

Add subject-aware trim / fill targeting

For shirt clipart-style art:

  • detect the true visible subject more aggressively

  • trim tighter than the current alpha bounds

  • then scale to a target shirt fill

Good next behavior:

  • trim based on visible non-background subject, not just loose alpha bounds

  • target a shirt front fill ratio for small central designs

  • keep the current max_upscale_factor cap for quality protection

What not to do

Do not just raise the shirt cap again right away.

Why:

  • this cat image is already capped at 4.0x

  • if the subject inside the file is still tiny, a bigger cap may just make it blurrier instead of solving the real problem

Recommended next Codex prompt

text
The latest shirt upscale cap works, but the cat shirt mockup proves the visible design itself is still too small. The issue is no longer just outer canvas size; the actual visible subject inside the source art is too small relative to the detected trim bounds.

Please implement the next safe enhancement for InkVibeAuto:

1. Add a more aggressive subject-aware trim mode for shirt front artwork.
2. This trim should detect the actual visible subject more tightly than the current loose alpha-bounds approach.
3. Support an optional mode for clipart/central-subject designs, while preserving current behavior for other artwork.
4. Add a shirt subject fill target so small central designs can scale to a better visual size after trimming.
5. Keep current max_upscale_factor protection in place.
6. Add logs/report fields showing:
   - subject bounds before aggressive trim
   - subject bounds after aggressive trim
   - subject fill target
   - whether aggressive trim was used
7. Preserve mugs unchanged.
8. Preserve contain mode.
9. Preserve current publish/verify/rebuild flows.

Also add tests proving:
- the cat-style small-subject case becomes visibly larger after aggressive trim
- mug behavior is unchanged
- current large-art cases still behave safely

For this one cat design, the fastest path is still: crop the source tighter by hand, rerun, and compare.


You said:
Summary Added shirt-specific aggressive subject trim controls to templates (aggressive_subject_trim_for_shirts, aggressive_subject_trim_mode, shirt_subject_fill_target) and extended processing/report dataclasses to carry aggressive-trim telemetry fields. Implemented a new aggressive subject-aware trim path using connected-component analysis (clipart_central) plus optional fill-target expansion, then integrated it into artwork resolution while preserving contain-mode flow and existing upscale-cap behavior. Scoped aggressive trim to shirt front placements only in export preparation (mugs/non-shirt templates remain unchanged), and kept existing shirt-only trim safeguards intact. Added template validation/loading for the new aggressive-trim fields and propagated aggressive-trim report values into run reporting outputs. Added tests covering: cat-style small-subject enlargement with aggressive trim, mug behavior unchanged, and safety of large-art with max_upscale_factor cap still enforced. Testing ✅ python -m py_compile printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py ✅ pytest -q tests/test_pipeline.py -k "aggressive_subject_trim_makes_small_central_subject_visibly_larger or aggressive_shirt_trim_flags_do_not_change_mug_behavior or aggressive_trim_still_respects_max_upscale_factor_for_large_art or template_level_shirt_only_trim_toggle_does_not_trim_mugs" ✅ pytest -q tests/test_pipeline.py -k "trim or contain_mode or max_upscale_factor or template_validation_rejects_invalid_trim" Committed and PR metadata recorded via make_pr. Files (2) printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py +211 -0 test_pipeline.py tests/test_pipeline.py +96 -0-https://github.com/infoinkvibe/inkvibe/pull/30

ChatGPT said:

PR #30 is merged, and it targets exactly the issue you showed: small central shirt designs like the cat mockup. It adds shirt-only aggressive subject trim, a clipart_central mode, optional fill targeting, and telemetry for before/after subject bounds, while preserving mug behavior and the existing upscale cap. GitHub

Run this next:

bash
git pull
python -m pytest -q

python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --max-artworks 9 \
  --stop-after-failures 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_nine_shirts_aggressive_trim.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_nine_shirts_aggressive_trim_failures.csv

What to look for in the cat row:

  • tighter subject bounds than before

  • aggressive trim being used

  • better fill on the shirt without removing contain mode

  • upscale cap still respected if needed

Then run the full combined regression:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --template-key mug_11oz \
  --max-artworks 9 \
  --stop-after-failures 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_nine_shirts_nine_mugs_aggressive_trim.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_nine_shirts_nine_mugs_aggressive_trim_failures.csv

PR #30 says mugs should remain unchanged and large-art safety should stay intact, so the main proof point is whether the cat shirt finally looks like the design itself is bigger, not just the outer box. GitHub

$ ...git pull python -m pytest -q python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \ --template-key tshirt_gildan \ --max-artworks 9 \ --stop-after-failures 1 \ --rebuild-product \ --publish \ --verify-publish \ --skip-audit \ --export-run-report reports/test_nine_shirts_aggressive_trim.csv \ --export-failure-report reports/test_nine_shirts_aggressive_trim_failures.csv bash: ...git: command not found ........................................................................ [ 64%] ....................................... [100%] 111 passed in 5.40s 2026-03-16 03:20:18,789 INFO [inkvibeauto] Loaded 1 template(s) and 9 artwork file(s) 2026-03-16 03:20:18,790 INFO [inkvibeauto] Processing artwork: ai-generated-9228632.png 2026-03-16 03:20:19,029 WARNING [inkvibeauto] Request GET /catalog/blueprints/6/print_providers/99/variants.json failed with HTTP 429 (1/5); retryable status, retrying in 523.00s--

ChatGPT said:

Two separate things happened.

First, this was a typo:

bash
...git pull

Bash tried to run a command literally named ...git, so git pull never happened.

Second, the shirt run hit a Printify rate limit:

text
HTTP 429 ... /catalog/blueprints/6/print_providers/99/variants.json

That means:

  • you did not actually pull the newest code first

  • the pipeline then started

  • Printify throttled the variant lookup

  • it is now trying to wait and retry

--skip-audit only skips the startup audit. It does not prevent every later catalog/variant API call.

What to do now

If it is still sitting on that long retry, stop it with Ctrl+C.

Then run this exactly:

bash
git pull
python -m pytest -q

python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --max-artworks 9 \
  --stop-after-failures 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_nine_shirts_aggressive_trim.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_nine_shirts_aggressive_trim_failures.csv

Important note

Because git pull did not run, this last attempt does not prove PR #30 is active.

So the right sequence is:

  1. stop the current stalled run

  2. run a real git pull

  3. run tests

  4. rerun shirts

If you want to be safer with rate limits, wait a few minutes before rerunning.

ChatGPT said:

PR #30 is pulled in now. The proof is:

  • Updating 48a3cd9..57c93fb

  • 114 passed in 6.30s

So the code is current.

The problem now is not the code. It’s Printify rate limiting:

text
HTTP 429 ... /catalog/blueprints/6/print_providers/99/variants.json
retrying in 370.00s

What that means

You’ve been doing a lot of live rebuild/publish runs back-to-back, and Printify is throttling the variant lookup call.

--skip-audit helps skip the startup audit, but it does not stop all later catalog calls.

Best move right now

If you do not want to wait 370 seconds, press:

bash
Ctrl+C

Then wait about 10–15 minutes before trying again.

Best next test after the cooldown

Since the main thing you want to verify is the cat shirt, don’t rerun all 9 first.

Run a smaller shirt test after the cooldown:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --max-artworks 2 \
  --stop-after-failures 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_two_shirts_aggressive_trim.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_two_shirts_aggressive_trim_failures.csv

Why --max-artworks 2:

  • artwork 1 is ai-generated-9228632.png

  • artwork 2 is the cat

  • that gets you to the cat much faster with fewer API calls

If you want to let the current run continue

You can also just let it sit and retry once the cooldown expires. The code itself is fine; the blocker is external rate limiting.

What to look for once it runs

For the cat row, you want to see signs that aggressive trim is actually changing the subject size, not just the outer box. After that, the real check is the mockup itself: whether the cat and text are finally visually larger on the shirt.

So at this moment:

  • repo status: good

  • tests: good

  • PR #30: present

  • blocker: Printify 429 rate limit

After a cooldown, rerun the 2-shirt test first instead of the full 9.

ChatGPT said:

The run succeeded, but the aggressive trim is not actually turned on yet.

Why I’m sure:

For the cat row, the log still shows the same loose trim as before:

  • original 500x500

  • trimmed 433x465

And then it only does the capped upscale:

  • requested_upscale_factor=10.393

  • applied_upscale_factor=4.000

What I do not see are any signs that the new PR #30 subject-aware trim path ran. If it had, the cat’s detected subject box should have been much tighter than 433x465, or there would be new aggressive-trim telemetry showing up.

The reason is likely simple:

  • PR #30 changed the code

  • but your summary for PR #30 only showed changes to:

    • printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py

    • tests/test_pipeline.py

  • it did not show product_templates.json changing

So the feature exists, but it looks like it is still off by default in your template.

What to do next

Open product_templates.json and turn the shirt aggressive trim on for tshirt_gildan.

Add these fields to the shirt template:

json
"aggressive_subject_trim_for_shirts": true,
"aggressive_subject_trim_mode": "clipart_central",
"shirt_subject_fill_target": 0.7

Start with 0.7.

If the cat is still too small after that, try:

  • 0.75

If it gets too large or cramped, back down to:

  • 0.65

Then rerun just the 2-shirt test

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --max-artworks 2 \
  --stop-after-failures 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_two_shirts_aggressive_trim.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_two_shirts_aggressive_trim_failures.csv

What you want to see in the next cat log

Instead of staying at:

  • trimmed=433x465

you want the cat row to show a much tighter subject box than before, plus signs that aggressive trim was used.

That should make the actual cat design larger on the shirt, not just enlarge the same loose box.

Bottom line

Your latest run proves:

  • the pipeline works

  • the upscale cap works

  • the cat is still too small

But it also strongly suggests:

  • PR #30 code is present

  • PR #30 template settings are not enabled yet

So the next real step is enable the aggressive shirt trim fields in product_templates.json, then rerun the 2-shirt test.


You said:
$ "aggressive_subject_trim_for_shirts": true, "aggressive_subject_trim_mode": "clipart_central", "shirt_subject_fill_target": 0.7 bash: aggressive_subject_trim_for_shirts:: command not found bash: aggressive_subject_trim_mode:: command not found bash: shirt_subject_fill_target:: command not found

ChatGPT said:

Those are JSON lines, not terminal commands.

Bash is trying to execute them, which is why you got:

  • command not found

You need to put those lines inside product_templates.json, in the tshirt_gildan template block.

What to do

Open the file:

bash
nano product_templates.json

Or open it in VS Code:

bash
code product_templates.json

Then find the tshirt_gildan object and add these fields inside it:

json
"aggressive_subject_trim_for_shirts": true,
"aggressive_subject_trim_mode": "clipart_central",
"shirt_subject_fill_target": 0.7

It should look something like this inside the shirt template object:

json
"key": "tshirt_gildan",
...
"artwork_fit_mode": "contain",
"allow_upscale": true,
"max_upscale_factor": 4.0,
"aggressive_subject_trim_for_shirts": true,
"aggressive_subject_trim_mode": "clipart_central",
"shirt_subject_fill_target": 0.7,
...

Make sure:

  • commas are correct

  • the JSON stays valid

  • you only add this to the shirt template, not the mug

After saving the file

Run:

bash
python -m pytest -q

Then rerun the 2-shirt test:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --max-artworks 2 \
  --stop-after-failures 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_two_shirts_aggressive_trim.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_two_shirts_aggressive_trim_failures.csv

If you want, paste the tshirt_gildan section of product_templates.json here and I’ll show you exactly where those three lines go.

ChatGPT said:

Yes — here’s the revised valid JSON.

The main correction is:

  • aggressive_subject_trim_for_shirts

  • aggressive_subject_trim_mode

  • shirt_subject_fill_target

should be on the tshirt_gildan template level, not inside the placements object.

json
[
  {
    "key": "tshirt_gildan",
    "printify_blueprint_id": 6,
    "printify_print_provider_id": 99,
    "title_pattern": "{artwork_title} {product_type_label}",
    "description_pattern": "{generated_description}<p>Best for {audience}. Style notes: {style_keywords}.</p>",
    "enabled_colors": [
      "Black",
      "White"
    ],
    "enabled_sizes": [
      "S",
      "M",
      "L",
      "XL"
    ],
    "tags": [
      "shirt",
      "graphic"
    ],
    "seo_keywords": [
      "graphic tee",
      "gift idea",
      "streetwear"
    ],
    "audience": "casual streetwear fans",
    "product_type_label": "T-Shirt",
    "style_keywords": [
      "minimal",
      "vintage"
    ],
    "shopify_product_type": "Apparel",
    "publish_to_shopify": false,
    "push_via_printify": true,
    "publish_after_create": true,
    "publish_title": true,
    "publish_description": true,
    "publish_images": true,
    "publish_variants": true,
    "publish_tags": true,
    "default_price": "29.99",
    "base_price": "24.00",
    "markup_type": "fixed",
    "markup_value": "5.00",
    "rounding_mode": "x_99",
    "compare_at_price": "34.99",
    "placements": [
      {
        "placement_name": "front",
        "width_px": 4500,
        "height_px": 5400,
        "allow_upscale": true,
        "placement_scale": 0.9,
        "placement_x": 0.5,
        "placement_y": 0.5,
        "placement_angle": 0,
        "artwork_fit_mode": "contain",
        "max_upscale_factor": 4.0
      }
    ],
    "trim_artwork_bounds": false,
    "trim_artwork_bounds_for_shirts": true,
    "trim_bounds_min_alpha": 8,
    "trim_bounds_padding_pct": 0.015,
    "trim_bounds_min_reduction_pct": 0.01,
    "aggressive_subject_trim_for_shirts": true,
    "aggressive_subject_trim_mode": "clipart_central",
    "shirt_subject_fill_target": 0.7,
    "max_upscale_factor": 4.0
  },
  {
    "key": "mug_11oz",
    "printify_blueprint_id": 68,
    "printify_print_provider_id": 1,
    "title_pattern": "{artwork_title} {product_type_label}",
    "description_pattern": "<p>{artwork_title} on a durable {product_type_label}.</p><p>Made for {audience}. Keywords: {seo_keywords}</p>",
    "enabled_colors": [],
    "enabled_sizes": [
      "11oz"
    ],
    "max_enabled_variants": 12,
    "enabled_variant_option_filters": {
      "size": [
        "11oz"
      ]
    },
    "tags": [
      "mug",
      "coffee"
    ],
    "seo_keywords": [
      "coffee mug",
      "desk accessory",
      "gift for coworkers"
    ],
    "audience": "coffee drinkers and office gifting",
    "product_type_label": "11oz Mug",
    "style_keywords": [
      "clean",
      "playful"
    ],
    "shopify_product_type": "Drinkware",
    "publish_to_shopify": false,
    "push_via_printify": true,
    "publish_after_create": true,
    "publish_title": true,
    "publish_description": true,
    "publish_images": true,
    "publish_variants": true,
    "publish_tags": true,
    "default_price": "17.99",
    "base_price": "12.00",
    "markup_type": "percent",
    "markup_value": "35",
    "rounding_mode": "whole_dollar",
    "placements": [
      {
        "placement_name": "front",
        "width_px": 2700,
        "height_px": 1120,
        "allow_upscale": false,
        "placement_scale": 0.78,
        "placement_x": 0.5,
        "placement_y": 0.5,
        "placement_angle": 0,
        "artwork_fit_mode": "contain"
      }
    ]
  }
]

ChatGPT said:

The new aggressive trim is running now, but the cat is still not big enough because the trim only got slightly tighter. I’m checking the source image itself so I can tell whether the remaining limit is the trim logic, the 4.0 upscale cap, or both.

This run tells us exactly what’s going on.

The aggressive trim is on now, but it is not the main limiter anymore.

What the log proves for the cat shirt

For the cat file:

  • Aggressive trim ... used=True

  • before: subject_bounds_before=(8, 8, 425, 457)

  • after: subject_bounds_after=(0, 48, 433, 465)

  • trimmed image became 433x417

So the aggressive trim only tightened the image a little.

Then the important line:

  • requested_upscale_factor=10.393

  • applied_upscale_factor=4.000

  • upscale_capped=True

That means:

The cat is still too small because the 4.0 upscale cap is now the thing stopping it, not the trim.

So the order is now:

  1. aggressive trim runs

  2. it helps a little

  3. pipeline wants to upscale the cat 10.393x

  4. cap stops it at 4.0x

  5. result is still too small on the shirt

Best next fix

For this kind of tiny central shirt art, the next change should be:

  • raise shirt max_upscale_factor

  • leave mugs unchanged

  • keep aggressive trim enabled

Recommended next value

Change shirt max_upscale_factor from:

json
"max_upscale_factor": 4.0

to:

json
"max_upscale_factor": 6.0

I would do that in both places for tshirt_gildan:

  • template level

  • front placement level

That gives you a stronger increase without jumping all the way to the full 10.393x.

Revised shirt template block

Use this for the tshirt_gildan section:

json
{
  "key": "tshirt_gildan",
  "printify_blueprint_id": 6,
  "printify_print_provider_id": 99,
  "title_pattern": "{artwork_title} {product_type_label}",
  "description_pattern": "{generated_description}<p>Best for {audience}. Style notes: {style_keywords}.</p>",
  "enabled_colors": [
    "Black",
    "White"
  ],
  "enabled_sizes": [
    "S",
    "M",
    "L",
    "XL"
  ],
  "tags": [
    "shirt",
    "graphic"
  ],
  "seo_keywords": [
    "graphic tee",
    "gift idea",
    "streetwear"
  ],
  "audience": "casual streetwear fans",
  "product_type_label": "T-Shirt",
  "style_keywords": [
    "minimal",
    "vintage"
  ],
  "shopify_product_type": "Apparel",
  "publish_to_shopify": false,
  "push_via_printify": true,
  "publish_after_create": true,
  "publish_title": true,
  "publish_description": true,
  "publish_images": true,
  "publish_variants": true,
  "publish_tags": true,
  "default_price": "29.99",
  "base_price": "24.00",
  "markup_type": "fixed",
  "markup_value": "5.00",
  "rounding_mode": "x_99",
  "compare_at_price": "34.99",
  "placements": [
    {
      "placement_name": "front",
      "width_px": 4500,
      "height_px": 5400,
      "allow_upscale": true,
      "placement_scale": 0.9,
      "placement_x": 0.5,
      "placement_y": 0.5,
      "placement_angle": 0,
      "artwork_fit_mode": "contain",
      "max_upscale_factor": 6.0
    }
  ],
  "trim_artwork_bounds": false,
  "trim_artwork_bounds_for_shirts": true,
  "trim_bounds_min_alpha": 8,
  "trim_bounds_padding_pct": 0.015,
  "trim_bounds_min_reduction_pct": 0.01,
  "aggressive_subject_trim_for_shirts": true,
  "aggressive_subject_trim_mode": "clipart_central",
  "shirt_subject_fill_target": 0.7,
  "max_upscale_factor": 6.0
}

Leave the mug template alone.

Then run just the 2-shirt test again

bash
python -m pytest -q

python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --max-artworks 2 \
  --stop-after-failures 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_two_shirts_aggressive_trim.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_two_shirts_aggressive_trim_failures.csv

What you want to see next

For the cat row, ideally:

  • requested_upscale_factor=10.393

  • applied_upscale_factor=6.000

  • upscale_capped=True

That will make it noticeably larger than the current 4.0x result.

Important tradeoff

Because the source image is only 500x500, raising the cap will make it bigger, but it may also get blurrier.

So the real tradeoff now is:

  • 4.0 = sharper but too small

  • 6.0 = bigger, maybe acceptable

  • 10.393 = probably too blurry

That is why 6.0 is the best next test.

If you want, after that run, paste the cat row again and I’ll tell you whether to stop at 6.0 or go a little higher.
