ChatGPT said:

The run itself worked, and the important part is that the logs show why the cat is still small.

This run confirms the problem is not the pipeline failing anymore. The problem is now where the final shirt size is being limited.

For the cat shirt, the important lines are:

  • Aggressive trim debug ... used=True

  • trimmed=433x417

  • requested_upscale_factor=10.393

  • applied_upscale_factor=6.000

  • resized=2598x2502

  • orientation=square scale=0.700

What that means:

The cat image did get extra shirt-specific trimming and did upscale more than before. But two things are still keeping it visually small:

  1. The crop still is not very tight

    • After all trimming, the usable cat art is only 433x417 before upscale.

    • That means the algorithm still thinks a lot of the image belongs to the subject area, so it is not cutting down to a much tighter cat-only box.

  2. The final shirt placement is still capped at square scale 0.700

    • This is the bigger blocker now.

    • Your template may say placement_scale: 0.9, but the log proves the real applied transform is still:

      • orientation=square scale=0.700

    • So the uploaded image can improve, but the shirt mockup still places it at the old square cap.

The key takeaway:

The cat is still too small mostly because the shirt square placement cap is still 0.700.
The image-prep side is helping now, but the final transform is still shrinking the result.

What I would change next:

  • Keep the aggressive trim settings on.

  • Keep shirt allow_upscale: true.

  • Keep the shirt upscale cap at 6.0 for now since it clearly helped.

  • Raise the shirt square placement cap in code, because that is now the main bottleneck.

The log proves that this is the choke point:

  • uploaded/exported art improved

  • aggressive trim ran

  • upscale increased

  • but final shirt transform still stopped at 0.700

So the next real fix is not more trimming first. It is:
increase the shirt square orientation cap above 0.700.

A good next target would be to raise square shirts to around 0.80 first, then test the same 2-shirt run again. If that still looks small, go a bit higher.

Right now the bottleneck order is:

  1. square shirt placement cap = biggest issue

  2. crop tightness = secondary issue

  3. upscale cap = already helping

Send me the current shirt orientation cap section from printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py, and I’ll give you the exact revised code block to change.


You said:
on printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py the code to that is 8251

ChatGPT said:

I’m checking that part of the file so I can point you to the exact block to change, not just guess.

Yep — that line area is likely the right spot.

From your logs, the shirt is still ending with:

  • orientation=square

  • scale=0.700

So the change you want is the shirt square orientation cap, not the mug logic.

Use this idea in the shirt transform section near that line:

python
def compute_placement_transform_for_artwork(
    placement: PlacementRequirement,
    artwork: Artwork,
    template_key: str,
) -> PlacementTransform:
    orientation = _orientation_bucket(artwork.image_width, artwork.image_height)

    scale = placement.placement_scale

    if template_key == "tshirt_gildan":
        shirt_orientation_caps = {
            "portrait": 0.72,
            "square": 0.80,   # was 0.70
            "landscape": 0.66,
        }
        scale = min(scale, shirt_orientation_caps.get(orientation, scale))

    elif template_key == "mug_11oz":
        mug_orientation_caps = {
            "portrait": 0.60,
            "square": 0.58,
            "landscape": 0.54,
        }
        scale = min(scale, mug_orientation_caps.get(orientation, scale))

    return PlacementTransform(
        scale=scale,
        x=placement.placement_x,
        y=placement.placement_y,
        angle=placement.placement_angle,
    )

If your code is not shaped exactly like that, the important change is simply:

python
"square": 0.80

instead of:

python
"square": 0.70

A good next test command after saving is:

bash
python -m pytest -q
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --max-artworks 2 \
  --stop-after-failures 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_two_shirts_squarecap.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_two_shirts_squarecap_failures.csv

What you want to see in the log for the cat is:

text
Placement transform template=tshirt_gildan placement=front orientation=square scale=0.800

If that still looks too small, move square from 0.80 to 0.84.

Also, since your current run shows:

  • aggressive trim used = True

  • applied_upscale_factor = 6.000

the next bottleneck is almost definitely that final scale=0.700, not the trim step anymore.

ChatGPT said:

That failure is just the test still expecting the old square value.

Your run shows the code is doing what you changed:

  • orientation=square scale=0.800

  • the cat shirt rebuilt successfully

  • pytest only fails because the test still says 0.70

Change this test in tests/test_pipeline.py:

python
def test_compute_placement_transform_for_shirt_uses_updated_orientation_caps():
    placement = PlacementRequirement("front", 4500, 5400, placement_scale=0.9)

    portrait = Artwork("p", Path("p.png"), "P", "", [], 1000, 1800)
    square = Artwork("s", Path("s.png"), "S", "", [], 1200, 1200)
    landscape = Artwork("l", Path("l.png"), "L", "", [], 1800, 1000)

    assert compute_placement_transform_for_artwork(placement, portrait, "tshirt_gildan").scale == 0.72
    assert compute_placement_transform_for_artwork(placement, square, "tshirt_gildan").scale == 0.80
    assert compute_placement_transform_for_artwork(placement, landscape, "tshirt_gildan").scale == 0.66

The old line:

python
assert compute_placement_transform_for_artwork(placement, square, "tshirt_gildan").scale == 0.70

should now be:

python
assert compute_placement_transform_for_artwork(placement, square, "tshirt_gildan").scale == 0.80

Your log confirms the code is already using 0.800, so this is a test update, not a pipeline bug.

Then run:

bash
python -m pytest -q

And if you want the pipeline to run only after tests pass, use:

bash
python -m pytest -q && python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --max-artworks 2 \
  --stop-after-failures 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_two_shirts_squarecap.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_two_shirts_squarecap_failures.csv

Based on your log, the cat is now definitely larger than before because the shirt square placement moved from 0.700 to 0.800.

ChatGPT said:

Looks good.

You now have a clean successful run end to end:

  • 114 passed

  • pipeline completed with successes=2 failures=0

  • both shirts were rebuilt, published, and verified

  • reports exported with rows=2

The important part for the cat shirt is this:

  • Aggressive trim debug ... used=True

  • requested_upscale_factor=10.393

  • applied_upscale_factor=6.000

  • upscale_capped=True

  • orientation=square scale=0.800

That means:

  • the pipeline detected the cat as a small central subject

  • aggressive trim actually kicked in

  • it tried to enlarge it a lot

  • the safety cap stopped it at 6.0

  • the shirt placement for square art is now using 0.800 instead of the older smaller square behavior

So this run confirms the code path is working the way your latest changes intended.

One note: the cat still hit the upscale cap, so the design got bigger, but not as big as the raw request would have made it. That is why the log shows:

  • requested 10.393

  • applied 6.000

So the current behavior is:

  • bigger than before

  • safer than unlimited enlargement

  • square art gets more shirt area than before

Your strongest proof line is this one:

text
Placement transform template=tshirt_gildan placement=front orientation=square scale=0.800

And this one:

text
Aggressive trim debug ... used=True

So at this point:

  • tests pass

  • pipeline pass is clean

  • cat shirt enlargement logic is active

  • square shirt placement change is active

The next real check is just visual confirmation in Printify mockups to decide whether 0.800 + cap 6.0 is the sweet spot, or whether you want the cat even larger.


You said:
in the prinfy mockups is good size image

ChatGPT said:

Yes — if the Printify mockups look good now, then the shirt image sizing is in a good place.

That means the current combo is doing what you wanted:

  • aggressive trim is helping small centered art

  • square shirt placement is larger

  • the upscale cap is still protecting against going too far

So for now, I would treat these settings as the new working baseline and keep them.

The next best move is to run a larger shirt batch and watch for edge cases like:

  • very tiny subjects

  • very wide images

  • very tall images

  • photos that should stay more conservative

If the mockups look right across a few more shirts, then this part is basically solved.


You said:
since i made local changes - lets push those to git.

ChatGPT said:

Use this from the repo root:

bash
git status

git add printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py product_templates.json tests/test_pipeline.py

git commit -m "Tune shirt sizing, aggressive trim, and square placement behavior"

git push origin main

If Git says there’s nothing to commit, your changes are already committed and you only need:

bash
git push origin main

If you want the safer branch version instead of pushing straight to main, use:

bash
git checkout -b fix/shirt-mockup-sizing
git add printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py product_templates.json tests/test_pipeline.py
git commit -m "Tune shirt sizing, aggressive trim, and square placement behavior"
git push -u origin fix/shirt-mockup-sizing

ChatGPT said:

That worked — your code changes were committed and pushed to main.

Commit pushed:
ee4c91d — Tune shirt sizing, aggressive trim, and square placement behavior

One important note: Git also showed leftover local files that were not included in the commit, including:

  • deleted image: images/openclipart-vectors-animal-1299106_1920-removebg-preview.png

  • untracked test/artifact files like GET, HTTP, prepared_shirt.applied_upscale_factor, state.backup.json, state.before_copy_test.json, images_all/, and several image files

So your pipeline/code/template/test changes are on GitHub, but your working folder still has cleanup to do.

To see it clearly again:

bash
git status

To remove only untracked junk files and folders:

bash
git clean -fd

To also restore tracked files you changed or deleted locally:

bash
git restore .

Be careful with those cleanup commands because they delete local work.

ChatGPT said:

I’m pulling together both sides: what the repo does now and what this chat changed step by step, then I’ll turn that into a handoff summary with concrete next moves.

I’ve got the repo unpacked and I’m tracing the main pipeline, templates, state/reporting, and the newer shirt-sizing work so the summary is grounded in the actual codebase.

Here’s a paste-ready handoff summary for a new chat.

InkVibeAuto: project summary and chat handoff

I checked both the uploaded local repo snapshot and the public GitHub repo. Publicly, the repo is infoinkvibe/inkvibe on the main branch, and the repo currently includes the core pipeline, templates, tests, examples, docs, and helper modules. The GitHub repo page describes InkVibeAuto as a Python automation pipeline that turns local artwork into fully configured Printify products and can optionally sync to Shopify. GitHub

What the project is

InkVibeAuto is a Python-based print-on-demand automation system. Its job is to:

  • read artwork files from a local folder

  • prepare print-ready exports for specific product placements

  • choose valid Printify variants from catalog data

  • upload artwork to Printify, either directly or through Cloudflare R2

  • create, update, rebuild, publish, and verify products in Printify

  • optionally push to Shopify

  • track everything in state.json

  • export run/failure CSV reports for auditing and resume behavior

That architecture is explicitly documented in the repo README: the main entrypoint is printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py, the pipeline reads images, validates placement constraints, uploads to Printify, optionally syncs to Shopify, and uses state.json for idempotency. GitHub

What is in the repo right now

From the local repo snapshot, the main files are:

  • printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py — the main orchestrator, currently the heart of the system

  • product_templates.json — the active product configuration layer

  • tests/test_pipeline.py — large regression suite

  • printify_ui_automation.py — Playwright-based semi-automation helper for Printify UI tasks

  • content_engine.py — title/metadata helper extraction

  • state_store.py — state compaction/migration/index helpers

  • r2_uploader.py — Cloudflare R2 upload support

  • README.md — setup, workflows, launch-plan docs, sizing docs, reporting docs

  • launch_plan_template.csv and launch_plan_real.csv — explicit launch-plan workflow artifacts

  • examples/ and docs/ — sidecar metadata examples, UI automation examples, state migration note

The public GitHub repo currently shows the same general structure, including docs, examples, images, tests, content_engine.py, printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py, printify_ui_automation.py, product_templates.json, r2_uploader.py, and state_store.py. GitHub

How the system works end to end

1. Template-driven product creation

The pipeline is template-first. A template defines:

  • Printify blueprint id

  • print provider id

  • title and description patterns

  • enabled colors and sizes

  • optional variant filters

  • pricing rules

  • placement requirements

  • publishing flags

  • SEO-like metadata fields

Right now, the local repo snapshot has two active working templates:

  • tshirt_gildan

  • mug_11oz

They are the two templates validated most heavily in this chat.

2. Artwork discovery and metadata

Artwork is discovered from IMAGE_DIR. The system also supports optional sidecar JSON files for richer listing metadata like title, description, tags, SEO keywords, audience, style keywords, theme, collection, occasion, and artist note. The README documents that sidecar metadata exists and that listing preview mode can render copy without creating products. GitHub

3. Image preparation

This is the part that got the most work in this chat.

The pipeline can now do all of the following before upload:

  • preserve aspect ratio with contain

  • optionally use cover

  • trim transparent whitespace

  • guard trimming so it does not over-trim or do meaningless trims

  • apply shirt-only aggressive subject trimming

  • optionally upscale undersized shirt art

  • cap upscale with a max factor

  • report requested vs applied upscale

  • generate placement QA previews

The README documents the contain/cover behavior, launch-plan workflow, and orientation-aware placement transform tuning. It also documents that shirts and mugs currently use different scaling defaults, with shirts using larger caps than mugs. GitHub

4. Printify catalog + variant selection

The pipeline includes read-only catalog exploration tooling:

  • list blueprints

  • list providers for a blueprint

  • inspect variants

  • recommend providers

  • bootstrap a starter template snippet

That workflow is documented in the repo and is important because it makes adding new products much safer than guessing ids. GitHub

5. Product lifecycle

Per artwork/template pair, the system can:

  • create if no product exists

  • update if state has a product id

  • rebuild if requested

  • auto-rebuild on incompatible update

  • publish

  • verify after publish

The README explicitly documents create/update/rebuild behavior and stale-id recovery. GitHub

6. State and reporting

State is keyed per artwork_slug:template_key. The pipeline tracks processed products, uploads, publish status, and compacted result info. Run and failure CSVs are exported. Launch-plan row ids and optional collection/campaign fields are carried into reporting. The README also says state handling is centralized in state_store.py and that legacy state rows still migrate. GitHub

What this chat accomplished

This chat was mostly about solving shirt image sizing and mockup appearance without breaking mug behavior.

Baseline that was already working

You already had:

  • shirt template working

  • mug template working

  • create/rebuild/publish/verify working

  • run/failure CSVs working

  • tests passing

You repeatedly ran successful live tests for:

  • 1 shirt

  • 1 mug

  • 9 shirts + 9 mugs

The core problem

The shirt mockups were technically valid, but some designs looked too small on shirts. The underlying issue was not always the canvas size itself — often it was the visible subject inside the canvas being too small because of:

  • transparent margins

  • conservative contain

  • no upscale

  • square artwork not filling enough of the mockup visually

Major phases of the fix

Phase 1: controlled shirt contain upscale

A merged PR on March 16, 2026 added controlled shirt contain upscale with effective upscale-factor reporting. That was PR #28. GitHub

This changed the behavior so shirts could upscale undersized artwork while mugs remained conservative.

Phase 2: shirt-only max upscale cap

A second merged PR on March 16, 2026 added shirt-only max_upscale_factor, plus reporting and regression tests. That was PR #29. GitHub

This prevented absurd enlargement while keeping shirts more flexible than mugs.

Phase 3: aggressive subject-aware trim

A third merged PR on March 16, 2026 added aggressive, subject-aware shirt trim with fill-target behavior and reporting. That was PR #30. GitHub

This was the key change for images like the cat, where the actual subject was too small even though the final file dimensions were fine.

The final local tuning done in this chat

After the PRs, you made additional local tuning and pushed it:

  • shirt max_upscale_factor increased from 4.0 to 6.0

  • shirt square orientation mockup cap increased from 0.70 to 0.80

  • aggressive subject trim remained enabled for shirts

  • tests were updated and eventually returned to green

  • the end result was that the Printify mockups looked good to you

Your local repo snapshot and run logs show that this is now behaving as intended:

  • the cat image used aggressive trim

  • requested upscale was 10.393

  • applied upscale was capped at 6.000

  • shirt square placement transform became 0.800

  • the Printify mockup looked like the design was finally large enough

You also got back to a clean test state with 114 passed.

Current working behavior

Shirt template

Current shirt behavior in the local repo snapshot is:

  • contain fit mode

  • shirt-only trim enabled

  • aggressive subject trim enabled

  • clipart_central trim mode

  • shirt subject fill target enabled

  • upscale allowed

  • shirt cap currently set to 6.0

  • square orientation mockup transform currently effectively allows 0.80

Mug template

Mug behavior remains intentionally conservative:

  • contain fit mode

  • no upscale by default

  • no shirt-only trim behavior applied

  • smaller placement transform caps

That split is good and should be preserved.

What collections status looks like right now

Collections have started, but only partially.

What exists now

There is already scaffolding for collection-related metadata:

  • collection_handle

  • collection_title

  • collection_description

  • launch_name

  • campaign

  • merch_theme

These are carried in launch-plan rows and run reports. Sidecar metadata also supports a plain collection field, and descriptions can reference collection context.

What does not exist yet

There is no real end-to-end collection system yet. In the current codebase I do not see:

  • Shopify collection creation

  • Shopify collection assignment

  • automated smart collection rules

  • collection image generation

  • collection landing-page generation

  • product-to-collection sync logic

So the answer is: collections are partially scaffolded as metadata/reporting, but not actually operational as a storefront organization workflow yet.

That should be one of the next major features.

Strengths of the project now

1. The core POD automation loop is real

This is no longer a mock project. It is doing real:

  • export prep

  • upload

  • product creation

  • publish

  • verify

  • stateful reruns

2. Good safety layers

The project has become much safer through:

  • variant safety limits

  • state-based resume

  • stale product-id recovery

  • update compatibility checks

  • optional rebuild modes

  • conservative mug behavior

  • reporting of upscale and trim telemetry

3. Strong troubleshooting tooling

You now have:

  • run reports

  • failure reports

  • launch plans

  • preview listing copy

  • placement previews

  • catalog inspection commands

  • UI automation helper for manual Printify follow-up flows

4. The shirt sizing issue is substantially solved

This was the biggest pain point in the chat, and now it is in a much better place.

Weak points / technical debt

1. Main pipeline file is too large

printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py is still the system brain and is very large. The repo has started modularizing into content_engine.py and state_store.py, but most logic still lives in the main file.

Recommendation: break it into modules:

  • catalog.py

  • templates.py

  • artwork_processing.py

  • publish.py

  • reporting.py

  • shopify_sync.py

  • launch_plan.py

2. Docs are drifting from code

This is important.

The local code/template snapshot now uses:

  • shirt square cap 0.80

  • shirt upscale cap 6.0

But the README still documents:

  • shirt square preset 0.70

  • conservative cap language from the older 4.0-era behavior

That mismatch should be fixed first.

3. Rate-limit handling needs improvement

You hit multiple 429 responses during this chat, including very long retry waits. The current retry logic exists, but product iteration is still too dependent on repeated live catalog fetches.

Recommendation: add a local catalog cache with TTL for:

  • blueprints

  • providers

  • variants

That would cut retries and make test runs much faster.

4. Collections are not finished

Metadata exists, but storefront collection workflows do not.

5. Titles, descriptions, and tags are still too template-simple

The project supports sidecars and better cleaning, but the copy system is still basic.

6. Repo hygiene needs work

The repo snapshot includes exports, images, reports, state files, virtualenv content, and older/legacy artifacts. This makes the repo heavier and noisier than it should be.

Suggestions for enhancement

Highest-priority next improvements

1. Finish collections properly

Build actual collection support, not just metadata.

Best path:

  • add collection config schema

  • create/update Shopify custom collections

  • attach products by collection_handle

  • optionally generate collection description and hero copy

  • add a collection run report

2. Improve titles, descriptions, and tags

This should be the next big commercial improvement.

Titles

Current titles are workable, but they should become:

  • cleaner

  • more specific

  • less generic

  • more SEO-aware

  • product-family-aware

Add:

  • title length guardrails

  • stopword cleanup

  • keyword ordering rules

  • niche-first patterns

  • product-label dedupe scoring

  • title scoring preview in dry-run

Descriptions

Current descriptions are still mostly template patterns plus fallback text.

Add:

  • product-specific description templates by category

  • tone presets

  • short/long description fields

  • first-paragraph hook

  • gift/use-case bullets

  • material/size/care copy blocks

  • niche keywords woven in naturally

Tags

This is a big opportunity.

Add a layered tag builder:

  • fixed template tags

  • artwork-sidecar tags

  • style tags

  • niche tags

  • holiday/seasonal tags

  • audience tags

  • product-type tags

  • search-intent tags

Also add:

  • tag normalization

  • singular/plural handling

  • dedupe

  • max tag counts per platform

3. Add catalog caching

This will reduce 429 pain and speed testing.

4. Refactor the pipeline

The project is now large enough to justify modular extraction.

5. Add a “product family” abstraction

Right now some behavior is keyed off template names like tshirt_gildan and mug_11oz.

Add explicit fields like:

  • product_family: shirt, mug, hoodie, poster, sticker

  • orientation_cap_profile

  • trim_profile

  • copy_profile

  • tag_profile

That will make it much easier to add products without stuffing special cases everywhere.

Best next products to add

Looking at current POD product trend and bestseller sources, the strongest next categories after tees and mugs are sweatshirts, hoodies, posters, stickers, phone cases, and tote bags. Printify’s current product-trend and bestseller content highlights custom t-shirts, sweatshirts, hoodies, mugs, stickers, phone cases, and posters/canvas as strong 2026 categories, while Printful’s bestseller/trend coverage also points to hoodies, sweatshirts, mugs, posters, and cases as strong performers. Printify+4Printify+4Printify+4

The best next two for this project

1. Crewneck sweatshirt

Why this should be next:

  • high-demand category

  • very close to shirt logic

  • same kind of front artwork placement workflow

  • likely reuses most of the shirt trimming/upscale/orientation behavior

  • low implementation risk

2. Pullover hoodie

Why this should be second:

  • another top seller

  • strong buyer demand

  • still close to current apparel workflow

  • good for higher average order value

  • same brand/niche art can reuse shirt assets

Best third/fourth after that

3. Poster / wall art

Why:

  • popular POD category

  • easy to create lifestyle cross-sell collections

  • less variant complexity than apparel

  • works well with artwork-heavy designs

4. Sticker

Why:

  • low-cost add-on item

  • strong impulse buy

  • great for bundling

  • easy niche expansion

What I would not prioritize immediately

  • phone cases: popular, but more variant/model complexity

  • candles: popular, but listing/copy/label workflows differ more

  • hats: popular, but embroidery/placement rules can diverge more

  • anything with many device/model variants too early

Recommended next implementation order

Step 1

Add:

  • crewneck_sweatshirt

  • pullover_hoodie

Use the existing catalog workflow to find valid blueprint/provider pairs instead of guessing. The repo already supports blueprint search, provider listing, provider recommendation, and template snippet generation. GitHub

Step 2

Create product-family presets:

  • shirt_like

  • mug_like

  • wall_art_like

  • sticker_like

Step 3

Build collections for:

  • animals

  • food/funny

  • floral/nature

  • abstract/symbolic

Step 4

Upgrade copy generation:

  • title strategy

  • description strategy

  • richer tags

Step 5

Add poster template

Concrete enhancement list for the next chat

Ask the next chat to do these in order:

  1. audit the repo for doc/code mismatches and update README to match actual current defaults

  2. extract shirt sizing logic into a cleaner product-family config system

  3. add crewneck_sweatshirt template with catalog-verified blueprint/provider

  4. add pullover_hoodie template with catalog-verified blueprint/provider

  5. make collections real in Shopify instead of only metadata

  6. redesign title/description/tag generation into layered copy profiles

  7. add catalog caching to avoid 429 delays

  8. clean repo hygiene: tighten .gitignore, separate fixtures from working assets, keep exports/reports/state backups out of source control

  9. optionally archive or clearly label printful_shopify_sync_pipeline.py as legacy so the repo is less confusing

Bottom line

This project has moved from “prototype” to “working POD operations tool.”

The most important outcome of this chat is:

  • shirts and mugs both work

  • the shirt image-size issue is largely solved

  • the cat/small-subject case is now much better

  • tests are back green

  • your latest local/git state was pushed

  • the next real frontier is catalog expansion + collections + better listing content

The smartest next products to add are crewneck sweatshirts and hoodies, because they are both commercially strong and technically closest to the shirt logic you already stabilized.


You said:
lets go to the next products add sweatshirts and hoodis

ChatGPT said:

I’m checking the repo structure and the built-in catalog/template tooling first so I can map sweatshirt/hoodie additions onto the workflow you already have.

Yes — sweatshirts first, then hoodies is the right next move.

Because your repo already has the catalog/discovery workflow built in, the safest way is:

  1. find the real Printify blueprint ids

  2. find the best provider for each

  3. inspect variants/placements

  4. generate starter template snippets

  5. tune them to match the shirt logic that now works well

Best order

Do these next:

  • crewneck sweatshirt

  • pullover hoodie

They are the closest to your working shirt flow, so they should be the easiest to add without breaking things.

Use this workflow now

1) Find sweatshirt blueprints

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py --list-blueprints --search-blueprints sweatshirt --limit-blueprints 15

2) Find hoodie blueprints

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py --list-blueprints --search-blueprints hoodie --limit-blueprints 15

3) For the sweatshirt blueprint you pick, rank providers

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py --recommend-provider --blueprint-id <SWEATSHIRT_BLUEPRINT_ID>

4) Inspect sweatshirt variants and placements

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py --inspect-variants --blueprint-id <SWEATSHIRT_BLUEPRINT_ID> --provider-id <SWEATSHIRT_PROVIDER_ID>

5) Generate a sweatshirt starter snippet

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py --generate-template-snippet \
  --blueprint-id <SWEATSHIRT_BLUEPRINT_ID> \
  --provider-id <SWEATSHIRT_PROVIDER_ID> \
  --key sweatshirt_basic \
  --template-output-file ./sweatshirt_template.json

6) Repeat for hoodie

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py --recommend-provider --blueprint-id <HOODIE_BLUEPRINT_ID>

python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py --inspect-variants --blueprint-id <HOODIE_BLUEPRINT_ID> --provider-id <HOODIE_PROVIDER_ID>

python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py --generate-template-snippet \
  --blueprint-id <HOODIE_BLUEPRINT_ID> \
  --provider-id <HOODIE_PROVIDER_ID> \
  --key hoodie_basic \
  --template-output-file ./hoodie_template.json

How to tune them after snippet generation

For both new templates, start from your working shirt behavior, not from mugs.

Use these settings as the starting pattern:

json
"artwork_fit_mode": "contain",
"allow_upscale": true,
"placement_scale": 0.9,
"placement_x": 0.5,
"placement_y": 0.5,
"placement_angle": 0,
"trim_artwork_bounds_for_shirts": true,
"aggressive_subject_trim_for_shirts": true,
"aggressive_subject_trim_mode": "clipart_central",
"shirt_subject_fill_target": 0.7,
"max_upscale_factor": 6.0

Then adjust based on mockups.

Recommended starter behavior

Sweatshirt

Start very close to your tee settings.

Suggested starting rules:

  • colors: Black, White

  • sizes: S, M, L, XL

  • fit mode: contain

  • allow upscale: true

  • aggressive trim: true

  • max upscale factor: 6.0

  • square orientation transform: start similar to the shirt behavior you now like

Hoodie

Start slightly more conservative than sweatshirt, because hoodies can visually crowd the chest area.

Suggested starting rules:

  • colors: Black, White

  • sizes: S, M, L, XL

  • fit mode: contain

  • allow upscale: true

  • aggressive trim: true

  • max upscale factor: 6.0

  • start with slightly smaller visual placement than sweatshirt if mockups look too big

Test sequence to use

After you paste both templates into product_templates.json:

Dry run one sweatshirt

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py --dry-run --template-key sweatshirt_basic --max-artworks 1

Dry run one hoodie

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py --dry-run --template-key hoodie_basic --max-artworks 1

Live test one sweatshirt

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key sweatshirt_basic \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_sweatshirt.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_sweatshirt_failures.csv

Live test one hoodie

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key hoodie_basic \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_hoodie.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_hoodie_failures.csv

What to look for in mockups

For each product, check:

  • is the design visually large enough on the chest

  • is square artwork too small

  • do portrait images look balanced

  • do landscape images feel too low-impact

  • does aggressive trim help small isolated subjects

  • does the 6.0 cap feel too soft or too aggressive

Your shirt work suggests this is the key split:

  • file size / canvas may be correct

  • but visible design subject size is what actually matters

So for sweatshirts and hoodies, judge the mockup visual size, not just the export dimensions.

My recommendation for template keys

Use:

  • sweatshirt_basic

  • hoodie_basic

Later, once you settle on exact blanks/models, rename them more specifically, like:

  • crewneck_gildan_18000

  • hoodie_gildan_18500

Only do that after you confirm the actual blueprint/provider pair you want to keep.

What I’d do next in the new chat

Paste back:

  • the blueprint search results

  • the provider recommendation results

  • the inspect-variants output for the sweatshirt

  • the inspect-variants output for the hoodie

  • your current product_templates.json

Then we can turn that into the exact finished sweatshirt and hoodie template entries.

ChatGPT said:

That output means the pipeline did a no-op:

  • 0 template(s) = hoodie_basic was not found in product_templates.json

  • 0 artwork file(s) = it also did not find any source images in the folder it is scanning

So the command itself ran, but there was nothing valid to process.

Use these checks from the repo root:

bash
python - <<'PY'
import json, pathlib

tpl_path = pathlib.Path("product_templates.json")
if tpl_path.exists():
    data = json.loads(tpl_path.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
    print("Template keys:")
    for row in data:
        print("-", row.get("key"))
else:
    print("product_templates.json not found")

img_dir = pathlib.Path("images")
if img_dir.exists():
    files = [p.name for p in img_dir.iterdir() if p.is_file()]
    print(f"\nImages found: {len(files)}")
    for name in files[:20]:
        print("-", name)
else:
    print("\nimages/ folder not found")
PY

If hoodie_basic is not listed, that is the first issue.

If you want a hoodie test, the template key must exactly match what is in product_templates.json, for example something like:

json
"key": "hoodie_gildan"

Then your command would need to use that exact key:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key hoodie_gildan \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_hoodie.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_hoodie_failures.csv

Also, if images are not being found, make sure you are running from the project root and that the files are in images/.

Fast diagnosis:

  • wrong or missing hoodie template key

  • running from the wrong folder

  • images/ empty or missing

  • template file changed but hoodie template was never actually added

Paste your current hoodie template block from product_templates.json, and I’ll turn it into the exact command to run.

bash

text

- tshirt_gildan

  1. Add a hoodie template to product_templates.json

Use this to confirm your current folder and image folder contents:

bash

import json
from pathlib import Path

data = json.loads(Path("product_templates.json").read_text(encoding="utf-8"))

    print(row.get("key"))
PY

Once you add the hoodie template, the command must use the exact key name you added. For example, if you add:

json

then run:

bash

ChatGPT said:

I’m sorting out the shell mistakes and turning those placeholder commands into exact ones you can paste. The big blockers are: angle-bracket placeholders were never replaced, and at that check your images/ folder was empty.

You’re very close. The failures were just shell/input mistakes, not a pipeline failure.

What went wrong:

  • hoodie_basic does not exist yet in product_templates.json

  • at that moment your images/ folder had 0 files

  • you typed the shell prompt character $

  • you used <PLACEHOLDER> literally, and Bash treated <...> as input redirection

That error:

bash
bash: SWEATSHIRT_BLUEPRINT_ID: No such file or directory

happens because this is wrong in Bash:

bash
--blueprint-id <SWEATSHIRT_BLUEPRINT_ID>

You must use a real number, with no < >.

Use these exact commands next.

First, get provider recommendations for the two best starting products:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py --recommend-provider --blueprint-id 49
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py --recommend-provider --blueprint-id 77

Recommended blueprint choices from your results:

  • 49 = Gildan crewneck sweatshirt

  • 77 = Gildan hoodie

Then inspect variants using the provider IDs returned by those commands.

Example format:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py --inspect-variants --blueprint-id 49 --provider-id 99
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py --inspect-variants --blueprint-id 77 --provider-id 99

Only use 99 there if the recommend-provider command actually returns 99. Use whatever real provider ID it gives you.

Then generate starter templates.

For sweatshirt:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py --generate-template-snippet \
  --blueprint-id 49 \
  --provider-id REAL_SWEATSHIRT_PROVIDER_ID \
  --key sweatshirt_gildan \
  --template-output-file ./sweatshirt_template.json

For hoodie:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py --generate-template-snippet \
  --blueprint-id 77 \
  --provider-id REAL_HOODIE_PROVIDER_ID \
  --key hoodie_gildan \
  --template-output-file ./hoodie_template.json

Then confirm your current templates and image count:

bash
python - <<'PY'
import json
from pathlib import Path

tpl = Path("product_templates.json")
if tpl.exists():
    data = json.loads(tpl.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
    print("Template keys:")
    for row in data:
        print("-", row.get("key"))

img_dir = Path("images")
if img_dir.exists():
    files = [p.name for p in img_dir.iterdir() if p.is_file()]
    print(f"\nImages found: {len(files)}")
    for name in files[:20]:
        print("-", name)
else:
    print("\nimages/ folder not found")
PY

Important:

  • do not type the leading $

  • do not type <...>

  • do not paste old log lines back into the terminal

Once you generate those two snippet files, paste them here and I’ll turn them into finished hoodie/sweatshirt templates that match your current shirt setup, including sizing, trim, titles, descriptions, and tags.

ChatGPT said:

You used the placeholder text literally again. Bash treats <HOODIE_BLUEPRINT_ID> like redirection, so it breaks.

Use this exact sequence.

First, find the hoodie provider for the Gildan hoodie blueprint 77:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py --recommend-provider --blueprint-id 77

Then take the real provider ID from that output and run:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py --inspect-variants --blueprint-id 77 --provider-id YOUR_PROVIDER_ID

Then generate the hoodie template:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py --generate-template-snippet \
  --blueprint-id 77 \
  --provider-id YOUR_PROVIDER_ID \
  --key hoodie_gildan \
  --template-output-file ./hoodie_template.json

Then verify the snippet file was created:

bash
ls hoodie_template.json

Right now this command fails:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key hoodie_gildan \
  ...

because hoodie_gildan is not yet in product_templates.json.

Also, your earlier log showed Loaded 0 template(s) and 0 artwork file(s), which means at that moment:

  • there was no hoodie_gildan template in product_templates.json

  • your images/ folder had no usable files

Check both with:

bash
python - <<'PY'
import json
from pathlib import Path

tpl = Path("product_templates.json")
if tpl.exists():
    data = json.loads(tpl.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
    print("Template keys:")
    for row in data:
        print("-", row.get("key"))

img_dir = Path("images")
if img_dir.exists():
    files = [p.name for p in img_dir.iterdir() if p.is_file()]
    print(f"\nImages found: {len(files)}")
    for name in files[:20]:
        print("-", name)
else:
    print("\nimages/ folder not found")
PY

So the immediate next command to run is:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py --recommend-provider --blueprint-id 77

Paste that output here, and I’ll turn it into the exact next hoodie commands with the real provider ID.

ChatGPT said:

Use 99 in place of YOUR_PROVIDER_ID.

Run these exact commands:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py --inspect-variants --blueprint-id 77 --provider-id 99

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py --generate-template-snippet \
  --blueprint-id 77 \
  --provider-id 99 \
  --key hoodie_gildan \
  --template-output-file ./hoodie_template.json

You got that error because YOUR_PROVIDER_ID was just a placeholder. Your actual hoodie provider is:

bash
provider_id=99

After that, hoodie_gildan still will not run until you add the generated hoodie template into product_templates.json.

Also, earlier your terminal showed:

bash
Images found: 0
Template keys:
- tshirt_gildan
- mug_11oz

So there are two separate reasons the hoodie run returned 0 template(s) and 0 artwork file(s):

  • hoodie_gildan is not yet in product_templates.json

  • your images/ folder was empty at that time

After generating the snippet, open hoodie_template.json, copy that object into product_templates.json, then test with:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key hoodie_gildan \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_hoodie.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_hoodie_failures.csv

Paste the --inspect-variants output and I’ll turn it into a ready-to-paste hoodie template.

ChatGPT said:

That last line:

bash
provider_id=99

did not run the pipeline. In Git Bash it just set a shell variable silently.

Do this next:

bash
ls -l hoodie_template.json
cat hoodie_template.json

If the file exists, copy that hoodie object into product_templates.json.

Then make sure you actually have images in images/, because earlier your terminal showed Images found: 0.

Then test the hoodie with:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key hoodie_gildan \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_hoodie.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_hoodie_failures.csv

If hoodie_template.json still does not exist, use this fallback so the snippet prints to the terminal instead:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --generate-template-snippet \
  --blueprint-id 77 \
  --provider-id 99 \
  --key hoodie_gildan

Then copy that printed JSON into product_templates.json.

If you want to use the shell variable you created, the command would be:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --generate-template-snippet \
  --blueprint-id 77 \
  --provider-id $provider_id \
  --key hoodie_gildan \
  --template-output-file ./hoodie_template.json

ChatGPT said:

I’ve got the hoodie snippet now. I’m turning it into a usable template instead of the raw generated version, keeping the first hoodie rollout simple and low-risk.

Good — the hoodie snippet was created successfully.

What happened before:

  • Loaded 0 template(s) happened because hoodie_gildan was not yet in product_templates.json.

  • Loaded 0 artwork file(s) happened because that shell session showed images/ was empty, so the pipeline had nothing to process.

The generated hoodie snippet is only a scaffold. It still needs real copy, safer color choices, placement tuning, and startup-friendly settings.

Paste this as a new object into product_templates.json:

json
{
  "key": "hoodie_gildan",
  "printify_blueprint_id": 77,
  "printify_print_provider_id": 99,
  "title_pattern": "{artwork_title} {product_type_label}",
  "description_pattern": "{generated_description}<p>Best for {audience}. Style notes: {style_keywords}.</p>",
  "enabled_colors": [
    "Black",
    "Dark Heather",
    "Charcoal",
    "Forest Green"
  ],
  "enabled_sizes": [
    "S",
    "M",
    "L",
    "XL",
    "2XL"
  ],
  "tags": [
    "hoodie",
    "graphic",
    "streetwear",
    "pullover"
  ],
  "seo_keywords": [
    "graphic hoodie",
    "pullover hoodie",
    "gift idea",
    "streetwear"
  ],
  "audience": "casual streetwear fans",
  "product_type_label": "Hoodie",
  "style_keywords": [
    "minimal",
    "cozy",
    "streetwear"
  ],
  "shopify_product_type": "Apparel",
  "publish_to_shopify": false,
  "push_via_printify": true,
  "publish_after_create": true,
  "publish_title": true,
  "publish_description": true,
  "publish_images": true,
  "publish_variants": true,
  "publish_tags": true,
  "default_price": "34.99",
  "base_price": "24.99",
  "markup_type": "fixed",
  "markup_value": "10.00",
  "rounding_mode": "x_99",
  "compare_at_price": "39.99",
  "placements": [
    {
      "placement_name": "front",
      "width_px": 4500,
      "height_px": 5400,
      "file_type": "png",
      "allow_upscale": true,
      "placement_scale": 0.80,
      "placement_x": 0.5,
      "placement_y": 0.5,
      "placement_angle": 0,
      "artwork_fit_mode": "contain",
      "max_upscale_factor": 4.0
    }
  ],
  "trim_artwork_bounds": true,
  "trim_bounds_min_alpha": 8,
  "trim_bounds_padding_pct": 0.015,
  "trim_bounds_min_reduction_pct": 0.01,
  "max_upscale_factor": 4.0
}

Why I revised it this way:

  • Front only first, because back and sleeve placements add complexity and more chances for bad sizing.

  • Provider 99 because your recommend-provider output showed it as the top match for blueprint 77.

  • Limited colors to keep the first rollout simple.

  • Contain + upscale cap matches the safer direction you already worked out for shirts.

  • trim_artwork_bounds: true helps remove transparent padding on hoodie art, even though the current aggressive shirt-only trim logic will not automatically apply to hoodies unless you expand that code later.

Then run:

bash
python -m pytest -q

If that passes, test one hoodie with:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key hoodie_gildan \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_hoodie.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_hoodie_failures.csv

If you still get Loaded 0 artwork file(s), point the pipeline at the folder that actually contains your images:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key hoodie_gildan \
  --image-dir images_all \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_hoodie.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_hoodie_failures.csv

For sweatshirts, the next best move is:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py --recommend-provider --blueprint-id 49

Then use the winning provider id to generate sweatshirt_gildan the same way.

What we want to see in the log:

  • Loaded 1 template(s)

  • Loaded 1 artwork file(s)

  • Variant selection template=hoodie_gildan

  • Placement transform ... placement=front

  • Printify publish completed

  • Printify verification ok

Then check the mockup for:

  • design size on chest

  • whether it sits too low or too high

  • whether placement_scale=0.80 feels right

If the hoodie art looks too small, the first tweak I’d make is:

  • placement_scale: 0.80 → 0.84

If it looks too large:

  • placement_scale: 0.80 → 0.76

Send me that hoodie run output and I’ll tune it from there.

$ python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \ --template-key hoodie_gildan \ --max-artworks 1 \ --rebuild-product \ --publish \ --verify-publish \ --skip-audit \ --export-run-report reports/test_one_hoodie.csv \ --export-failure-report reports/test_one_hoodie_failures.csv 2026-03-16 23:29:25,902 INFO [inkvibeauto] Loaded 0 template(s) and 0 artwork file(s) 2026-03-16 23:29:25,903 INFO [inkvibeauto] Failure report exported path=reports/test_one_hoodie_failures.csv rows=0 2026-03-16 23:29:25,903 INFO [inkvibeauto] Run report exported path=reports/test_one_hoodie.csv rows=0 2026-03-16 23:29:29,963 INFO [inkvibeauto] Run summary artworks_scanned=0 templates_processed=0 combinations_processed=0 successes=0 failures=0 skipped=0 products_created=0 products_updated=0 products_rebuilt=0 products_skipped=0 template_failures=0 publish_attempts=0 publish_verified=0 verification_warnings=0 2026-03-16 23:29:29,963 INFO [inkvibeauto] Done

ChatGPT said:

That run means two things at once:

  1. Loaded 0 template(s)
    hoodie_gildan is not in the template file the script is using by default.

  2. Loaded 0 artwork file(s)
    the script is looking in the default images/ folder, and that folder is empty.

Use the hoodie template file you generated, and point to the folder that actually has artwork:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --templates hoodie_template.json \
  --template-key hoodie_gildan \
  --image-dir images_all \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_hoodie.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_hoodie_failures.csv

If your artwork is in a different folder, swap images_all for that folder.

Why this happened:

  • default template source = product_templates.json

  • current keys there were only your older templates

  • hoodie was generated into hoodie_template.json

  • default image source = images/

  • your earlier check showed Images found: 0

To verify before running, use:

bash
python - <<'PY'
import json, pathlib

tpl = pathlib.Path("hoodie_template.json")
if tpl.exists():
    data = json.loads(tpl.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
    print("Template keys:", [row.get("key") for row in data] if isinstance(data, list) else data.get("key"))
else:
    print("hoodie_template.json not found")

for folder in ["images", "images_all"]:
    p = pathlib.Path(folder)
    if p.exists():
        files = [x.name for x in p.iterdir() if x.is_file()]
        print(folder, len(files))
PY

If you want hoodie_gildan to work without --templates hoodie_template.json, move that hoodie entry into product_templates.json.


You said:
$ python - <<'PY' import json, pathlib tpl = pathlib.Path("hoodie_template.json") if tpl.exists(): data = json.loads(tpl.read_text(encoding="utf-8")) print("Template keys:", [row.get("key") for row in data] if isinstance(data, list) else data.get("key")) else: print("hoodie_template.json not found") for folder in ["images", "images_all"]: p = pathlib.Path(folder) if p.exists(): files = [x.name for x in p.iterdir() if x.is_file()] print(folder, len(files)) PY Template keys: hoodie_gildan images 0

ChatGPT said:

Good — that confirms the template file is fine.

What it shows:

  • hoodie_template.json exists

  • key = hoodie_gildan

  • images has 0 files

  • images_all did not print, so it likely does not exist in this folder

So the hoodie run failed because the script had:

  • a valid hoodie template file

  • no artwork folder with files

Use the folder that actually contains your images. First find it:

bash
find . -maxdepth 2 -type f \( -iname "*.png" -o -iname "*.jpg" -o -iname "*.jpeg" \)

Or on Git Bash, to just check likely folders:

bash
ls images
ls images_all
ls .

Once you know where the artwork files are, run:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --templates hoodie_template.json \
  --template-key hoodie_gildan \
  --image-dir YOUR_IMAGE_FOLDER \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_hoodie.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_hoodie_failures.csv

Example, if the files are in the project root:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --templates hoodie_template.json \
  --template-key hoodie_gildan \
  --image-dir . \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_hoodie.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_hoodie_failures.csv

If you want to make hoodie work like shirts and mugs without --templates hoodie_template.json, add the hoodie template into product_templates.json.


You said:

ChatGPT said:

That avocado art looks good for this pipeline:

  • transparent background

  • centered subject

  • clean edges

  • square format, which should work well for shirts and hoodies

Your hoodie run failed earlier because your local images/ folder was empty, not because the artwork was bad.

Use this workflow on your computer:

bash
mkdir -p images

Put the avocado PNG into your local project images/ folder, then run:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --templates hoodie_template.json \
  --template-key hoodie_gildan \
  --image-dir images \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_hoodie.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_hoodie_failures.csv

If you want to test the same avocado on a shirt first:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --template-key tshirt_gildan \
  --image-dir images \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_avocado_shirt.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_avocado_shirt_failures.csv

One important note: your hoodie template is in hoodie_template.json, not product_templates.json, so you must include --templates hoodie_template.json unless you merge that hoodie template into the main template file.


You said:
$ $ python - <<'PY' import json, pathlib tpl = pathlib.Path("hoodie_template.json") if tpl.exists(): data = json.loads(tpl.read_text(encoding="utf-8")) print("Template keys:", [row.get("key") for row in data] if isinstance(data, list) else data.get("key")) else: print("hoodie_template.json not found") for folder in ["images", "images_all"]: p = pathlib.Path(folder) if p.exists(): files = [x.name for x in p.iterdir() if x.is_file()] print(folder, len(files)) PY Template keys: hoodie_gildan images 0 mkdir -p images python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \ --templates hoodie_template.json \ --template-key hoodie_gildan \ --image-dir images \ --max-artworks 1 \ --rebuild-product \ --publish \ --verify-publish \ --skip-audit \ --export-run-report reports/test_one_hoodie.csv \ --export-failure-report reports/test_one_hoodie_failures.csv bash: $: command not found bash: Template: command not found bash: images: command not found Traceback (most recent call last): File "C:\Projects\InkVibeAuto\printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py", line 3854, in <module> run( ~~~^ pathlib.Path(args.templates), ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ ...<50 lines>... placement_preview=args.placement_preview, ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ ) ^ File "C:\Projects\InkVibeAuto\printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py", line 3622, in run templates = load_templates(config_path) File "C:\Projects\InkVibeAuto\printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py", line 2277, in load_templates raise TemplateValidationError("product_templates.json must contain a top-level JSON array") TemplateValidationError: product_templates.json must contain a top-level JSON array

ChatGPT said:

Two separate problems happened.

First, Git Bash tried to run pasted output as commands:

  • bash: $: command not found

  • bash: Template: command not found

  • bash: images: command not found

That means you pasted the prompt/output text too. Only run the raw commands, not the $ lines or printed results.

Second, hoodie_template.json is a single JSON object, but your loader expects a top-level JSON array. That is why you got:

text
TemplateValidationError: product_templates.json must contain a top-level JSON array

Use this exact fix.

Step 1: convert hoodie_template.json into an array file the pipeline can load:

bash
python - <<'PY'
import json
from pathlib import Path

p = Path("hoodie_template.json")
data = json.loads(p.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))

if isinstance(data, dict):
    data = [data]

p.write_text(json.dumps(data, indent=2), encoding="utf-8")
print("Fixed hoodie_template.json to top-level JSON array.")
PY

Step 2: make sure your local images/ folder actually has an image in it. Right now your earlier output showed images 0, so the pipeline has nothing to process.

Step 3: run the hoodie test with only these commands:

bash
mkdir -p images

Then after you place your avocado PNG into images/, run:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --templates hoodie_template.json \
  --template-key hoodie_gildan \
  --image-dir images \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_hoodie.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_hoodie_failures.csv

Quick checks before rerunning:

bash
python - <<'PY'
import json
from pathlib import Path

tpl = Path("hoodie_template.json")
imgs = Path("images")

data = json.loads(tpl.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
print("template_type:", type(data).__name__)
print("template_keys:", [row.get("key") for row in data] if isinstance(data, list) else "not-a-list")

files = [p.name for p in imgs.iterdir() if p.is_file()] if imgs.exists() else []
print("images_count:", len(files))
print("images:", files[:10])
PY

You want to see:

  • template_type: list

  • template_keys: ['hoodie_gildan']

  • images_count: 1 or more

Right now the blocker is not Printify. It is:

  1. hoodie template file format

  2. empty images/ folder

Paste the output from the check block after you run it.

print("template_type:", type(data).__name__)

files = [p.name for p in imgs.iterdir() if p.is_file()] if imgs.exists() else []

ChatGPT said:

That confirms the pipeline is fine now. The only issue is:

images_count: 0

So there is still no local artwork file inside C:\Projects\InkVibeAuto\images\.

The image from chat does not automatically get saved into your project folder on your computer. You need to save the avocado image to your PC, then move or copy it into images.

Use one of these methods.

Manual method:

  1. Save the avocado PNG from chat to your computer.

  2. Put it in:

text
C:\Projects\InkVibeAuto\images\

Git Bash copy example from Downloads:

bash
cp "/c/Users/morga/Downloads/avocado.png" images/

Or from Desktop:

bash
cp "/c/Users/morga/Desktop/avocado.png" images/

Then verify it worked:

bash
python - <<'PY'
from pathlib import Path
imgs = Path("images")
files = [p.name for p in imgs.iterdir() if p.is_file()] if imgs.exists() else []
print("images_count:", len(files))
print("images:", files)
PY

You want to see something like:

text
images_count: 1
images: ['avocado.png']

Then run the hoodie test:

bash
python printify_shopify_sync_pipeline.py \
  --templates hoodie_template.json \
  --template-key hoodie_gildan \
  --image-dir images \
  --max-artworks 1 \
  --rebuild-product \
  --publish \
  --verify-publish \
  --skip-audit \
  --export-run-report reports/test_one_hoodie.csv \
  --export-failure-report reports/test_one_hoodie_failures.csv

At this point, the script is not broken. It just has no image file to process yet.
